# Spike Control Policy

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><tbody><tr><td><p><strong>Policy Name</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Spike Control</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Summary</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Regulates API traffic</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Category</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Quality of Service</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>First Omni Gateway version available</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>v1.0.0</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td rowspan="4"><p><strong>Returned Status Codes</strong></p></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><div><div><p>429 - The number of requests by HTTP APIs exceeded the configured limit. Request rejected after specified number of reattempts.</p></div></div></td></tr></tbody></table>

## Summary

The Spike Control policy regulates your API request traffic by limiting the number of messages
processed by an API. The policy ensures that the number of messages processed within a specified
time does not exceed the limit that you configure. If the number is exceeded, the request is queued
for retry based on you have configured the policy.

## Configuring Policy Parameters

The Spike Control policy does not perform quota enforcement. The configuration parameters are
restricted to protect the API and backend. Therefore, to ensure maximum performance, configure these
parameters to the lowest possible value.

### Omni Gateway Local Mode

When you apply the policy via declarative configuration files, Refer to the following policy
definition and table of parameters:

\- policyRef: name: spike-control-flex config: maximumRequests: <int> // REQUIRED, default: 1
timePeriodInMilliseconds: <int> // REQUIRED, default: 1000 delayTimeInMillis: <int> // REQUIRED,
default: 1000 delayAttempts: <int> // REQUIRED, default: 1 queuingLimit: <int> // OPTIONAL, default:
0 exposeHeaders: <bool> // OPTIONAL, default: false

<table><colgroup><col> <col> <col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Parameter</th><th>Required or Optional</th><th>Default Value</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><div><div><p><code>maximumRequests</code></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Required</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>1</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Number of allowed requests in the time window</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><div><div><p><code>timePeriodInMilliseconds</code></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Required</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>1000</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Time window size</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><div><div><p><code>delayTimeInMillis</code></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Required</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>1000</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>The amount of time for which each request is retained before retrying, in the event there is no quota remaining</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><div><div><p><code>delayAttempts</code></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Required</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>1</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>The number of times a request is retried before it is rejected</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><div><div><p><code>queuingLimit</code></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Optional</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>0</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>The number of requests that can be queued at the any given time</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><div><div><p><code>exposeHeaders</code></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Optional</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p><code>false</code></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Enable it only for internal APIs. Allows the policy to return information about the algorithm behavior in the X-RateLimit headers</p></div></div></td></tr></tbody></table>

#### Resource Configuration Example

\- policyRef: name: spike-control-flex config: queuingLimit: 0 exposeHeaders: true
delayTimeInMillis: 1000 timePeriodInMilliseconds: 1000 delayAttempts: 1 maximumRequests: 100000

### Managed Omni Gateway and Omni Gateway Connected Mode

When you apply the policy from the UI, the following parameters are displayed:

<table><colgroup><col> <col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Element</th><th>Description</th><th>Required</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><div><div><p>Number of Reqs</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>The number of requests allowed (in milliseconds) in the specified window</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Yes</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><div><div><p>Time Period</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>The number of milliseconds, within which a request must be processed</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Yes</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><div><div><p>Delay Time in Milliseconds</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>The amount of time for which each request is retained before retrying (in milliseconds) in case there is no quota remaining</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Yes</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><div><div><p>Delay Attempts</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>The number of times a request is retried before it is rejected</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Yes</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><div><div><p>Queuing Limit</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>The number of requests that can be queued at the any given time</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>No</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><div><div><p>Expose Headers</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Enable it only for internal APIs, allows the policy to return information about the algorithm behavior in the X-RateLimit headers</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>No</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><div><div><p>Method &amp; Resource conditions</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>The option to add configurations to only a select few or all methods and resources of the API</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Yes</p></div></div></td></tr></tbody></table>

To enforce request quotas, see the
[Rate Limiting: SLA-Based Policy](policies-included-rate-limiting-sla) and
[Rate Limiting Policy](policies-included-rate-limiting).

## How This Policy Works

The Spike Control policy uses a _Sliding Window Algorithm_ which reduces spikes in traffic and
protects the gateway instance. The window self-adjusts its display size to accommodate results over
time.

If the current window has no remaining request quota, without closing the connection to the client,
the Spike Control policy allows requests to be queued for processing later. For your policy to queue
the requests, you must configure the policy parameters, as shown in the following example:

- `Time Period`: 1 second
- `Number of Reqs`: 2
- `Delay Time in Milliseconds`: 499 milliseconds
- `Delay Attempts`: 1
- `Queuing Limit`: 5

Queuing a request requires retaining a thread and an HTTP connection. When you specify a
`Queuing Limit` for the policy, the parameter protects the gateway from running out of resources and
ensures that the API does not fail in case of an attack.

## Request Timelines

The following diagram illustrates the lifespan of each request accepted by the algorithm, using the
configuration defined in the previous section:

![A timeline that shows accepted, rejected, and queued requests](_images/spike-control.png)

1.  The first two requests are accepted and the available quota is now 0.

    The quota increases to 1 after one second (the `window size` value) passes from request #1.

2.  Request #3 arrives.

    Because no quota is available, the request is queued for 499 milliseconds.

3.  Request #4 arrives.

    Because there still is no quota available, the request is queued for 499 milliseconds.

4.  Shortly before request #3 is retried, request #1 ages a second and its quota is released because
    it falls out of the window.
5.  Quota is now 1, and request #3 is reprocessed successfully.

    Quota returns to 0 until request #2 ages 1 second.

6.  Request #4 delay concludes and it must be reprocessed.

    Request #4 is rejected. Requests #2 and #3 have not aged a second yet and there is no quota
    available. Request #4 have no more delays.

7.  Request #5 arrives.

    Because request #2 has already aged more than a second, the request is accepted.

This example illustrates request delays and how queuing regulates spikes in traffic. In
high-contention scenarios, the backend continues to function properly (it serves no more than X
requests in the last Y milliseconds).

Users querying the API might still see their requests being served, but with higher latency. The
exact configuration for the policy depends exclusively on your API and its throughput.

## FAQ

**When does the sliding window start?**

The window starts with the first request after the policy is successfully applied.

**What type of window does the algorithm use?**

The algorithm uses a sliding window that monitors transactions for the amount of time that you
configure in the policy.

**What happens when the quota is exhausted?**

When the request quota is exhausted, the Spike Control policy queues the request and retries as
configured. The request is rejected if no quota is remaining after several retries. Quota for other
requests is available only after the first request exceeds the time limit configured in the policy;
in this way, requests are processed in cycles.

**Can I configure multiple windows?**

No. The Spike Control policy ensures that the backend server does not serve more requests than it
can handle. If you need accountability, use either Rate-Limiting SLA or Rate Limit policies instead.

**What does each response header mean?**

Each response header has information about the current state of the request:

- X-Ratelimit-Remaining: The amount of quota currently available
- X-Ratelimit-Limit: The maximum available requests per window
- X-Ratelimit-Reset: The remaining time, in milliseconds, until the oldest request ages enough to
  fall outside of the sliding window.

  If quota is still available, the header is set to 0 (zero) because the algorithm can still serve
  new requests.

By default, the X-RateLimit headers are disabled in the response. You can enable these headers by
selecting **Expose Headers** when you configure the policy.

**When should I use Rate Limiting instead of Rate Limiting SLA or Spike Control?**

Use Rate Limiting and Rate-Limiting SLA policies for accountability and to enforce a hard limit to a
group (using the identifier in Rate Limiting) or to a client application (using Rate Limiting-SLA).
If you want to protect your backend, use the Spike Control policy instead.
---

# Stream Idle Timeout Policy

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><tbody><tr><td><p><strong>Policy Name</strong></p></td><td><p>Stream Idle Timeout</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Summary</strong></p></td><td><p>Sets the maximum duration a stream can remain idle without receiving a request or response</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Category</strong></p></td><td><p>Quality of Service</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>First Omni Gateway version available</strong></p></td><td><p>v1.9.0</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Returned Status Codes</strong></p></td><td><p>504 - Stream timeout</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

## Summary

The Stream Idle Timeout policy sets a maximum duration for which a stream can remain idle without
receiving any data, either inbound from the client to Omni Gateway or outbound from Omni Gateway to
the upstream service. The policy prevents idle connections from consuming resources unnecessarily.
The timeout period starts at the last activity on the stream, whether the activity is a request or a
response.

By default, all Omni Gateway API instances have a stream timeout of 300 seconds. Apply the policy to
override the default timeout.

## Configuring Policy Parameters

### Omni Gateway Local Mode

When you apply the policy via declarative configuration files, Refer to the following policy
definition and table of parameters:

\- policyRef: name: stream-idle-timeout-flex config: timeout: number // REQUIRED

<table><colgroup><col> <col> <col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Parameter</th><th>Required or Optional</th><th>Default Value</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><div><div><p><code>timeout</code></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Required</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>300</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Time in seconds before Omni Gateway closes the connection.</p></div></div></td></tr></tbody></table>

#### Resource Configuration Example

\- policyRef: name: stream-idle-timeout-flex config: timeout: 3

### Managed Omni Gateway and Omni Gateway Connected Mode

When you apply the policy from the UI, the following parameters are displayed:

<table><colgroup><col> <col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Element</th><th>Description</th><th>Required</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><div><div><p>Stream idle timeout</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Time in seconds before Omni Gateway closes the connection.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Yes</p></div></div></td></tr></tbody></table>
---

# Transport Layer Security Policy - Outbound

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><tbody><tr><td><p><strong>Policy Name</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Transport Layer Security (TLS) - Outbound</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Summary</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Enables two-way authentication between the API proxy and an upstream service</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Category</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Security</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>First Omni version available</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>v1.4.0</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td rowspan="4"><p><strong>Returned Status Codes</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>No return codes exist for this policy.</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><div></div></td></tr></tbody></table>

> [!NOTE] The following document applies only to Omni Gateway running in Local Mode. To configure
> TLS in Connected Mode, see
> [Configuring TLS Context for Omni Gateway in Connected Mode](flex-conn-tls-config).

## Summary

Omni Gateway supports outbound Transport Layer Security (TLS) and outbound mutual authentication TLS
(mTLS) in Local Mode.

By default, Omni Gateway supports outbound TLS to communicate with upstream services that require a
secure communication channel. For default outbound communication, Omni Gateway uses only TLS 1.2. To
find the default TLS 1.2 ciphers, see [Omni Gateway Supported Ciphers](#supported-ciphers). To find
the ciphers, see [Omni Gateway Supported Ciphers](#supported-ciphers). Applying a TLS context to an
upstream overrides the default TLS context for that upstream. Because the default ciphers may change
for later Omni Gateway versions, you can apply a TLS context to an upstream to ensure it remains the
same.

To apply outbound TLS in Local Mode, use the
[Configuring TLS Context for Omni Gateway in Local Mode](flex-local-tls-config) tutorial and refer
to the following configuration files in this documentation.

You can configure an outbound TLS context to enable authentication between an API instance and an
upstream service by binding the TLS policy to the upstream service.

Unlike inbound TLS, it is not suggested to apply the same outbound TLS context to all API instances
in your Omni Gateway because your API upstream services might come from different providers.
Therefore, no configuration example for outbound TLS on all instances is provided.

Port sharing is not a concern for outbound TLS. You can apply an outbound TLS context for each
individual upstream service.

> [!NOTE] TLS policies do not support policy ordering.

> [!NOTE] Upstream certificates must include the Subject Alternative Name (SAN) extension. The
> Common Name (CN) field is deprecated.
>
> Omni Gateway supports the SAN extension of type `dNSName`.

> [!NOTE] The Authority Information Access (AIA) certificate extension is not supported when
> configuring outbound TLS contexts.

## Configuring Policy Parameters

To enable outbound TLS between an API instance and an upstream service when using Omni Gateway as
your runtime, you must manually configure the TLS context in a YAML configuration file.

Refer to the following policy definition and table of parameters:

\- policyRef: name: tls-outbound config: skipValidation: <boolean> // REQUIRED certificate: //
OPTIONAL key: <string> // OPTIONAL crt: <string> // OPTIONAL alpn: <array> // OPTIONAL minversion:
<string> // OPTIONAL maxversion: <string> // OPTIONAL ciphers: <array> // OPTIONAL

Not including optional parameters in your configuration file applies the parameters default values
to your TLS context.

When configuring the `ciphers` parameter, ensure that your API upstream supports the listed ciphers.
For the supported ciphers, see [TLS Cipher Support on Omni Gateway](#local-support).

<table><colgroup><col> <col> <col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Parameter</th><th>Required or Optional</th><th>Default Value</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><div><div><p><code>skipValidation</code></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Required</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>false</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>If true, the upstream certificate is not validated.</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><div><div><p><code>trustedCA</code></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Optional, unless <code>skipValidation</code> is false</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>N/A</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>The CA used to validate the upstream server certificate. If no trusted CA is provided, the policy uses the default OS CA.</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><div><div><p><code>certificate</code></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Optional</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>N/A</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>The client certificate to present for mTLS. To comply with security standards, all certificates must be 2048 bits or longer.</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><div><div><p><code>certificate.key</code></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Optional</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>N/A</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>The private key part of the certificate.</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><div><div><p><code>certificate.crt</code></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Optional</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>N/A</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>The public key part of the certificate.</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><div><div><p><code>alpn</code></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Optional</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p><code>h2</code> and <code>http/1.1</code></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>A prioritized list of supported application level protocols; for example, h2, http/1.1, and so forth.</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><div><div><p><code>minversion</code></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Optional</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p><code>1.2</code></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>The minimum TLS version allowed</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><div><div><p><code>maxversion</code></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Optional</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p><code>1.3</code></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>The maximum TLS version allowed</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><div><div><p><code>ciphers</code></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Optional</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>For the default and other supported ciphers, see <a href="#local-support">TLS Cipher Support on Omni Gateway</a>.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>A list of supported TLS ciphers (IANA format).</p></div></div></td></tr></tbody></table>

### Resource Configuration Examples

You can format the YAML file to configure TLS or mTLS for a upstream service.

**Sample configuration for adding outbound TLS context for a specific upstream service:**

```yaml
apiVersion: gateway.mulesoft.com/v1alpha1
kind: PolicyBinding
metadata:
  name: tls-outbound
spec:
  targetRef:
    kind: Service
    name: ingress-test-outbound-upstream
  policyRef:
    name: tls-outbound
  config:
    skipValidation: true
    certificate:
      key: |
        # -----BEGIN PRIVATE KEY-----
        # insert certificate key
        # -----END PRIVATE KEY-----
      crt: |
        # -----BEGIN CERTIFICATE-----
        # insert certificate
        # -----END CERTIFICATE-----
```

This example uses the default values for `alpn`, `minversion`, `maxversion`, and `ciphers`.

**Sample configuration for adding outbound mTLS context for a specific upstream service:**

```yaml
apiVersion: gateway.mulesoft.com/v1alpha1
kind: PolicyBinding
metadata:
  name: mtls-outbound
spec:
  targetRef:
    kind: Service
    name: ingress-test-outbound-upstream
  policyRef:
    name: tls-outbound
  config:
    skipValidation: false
    trustedCA: |
      # -----BEGIN CERTIFICATE-----
      # insert certificate
      # -----END CERTIFICATE-----
    certificate:
      key: |
        # -----BEGIN PRIVATE KEY-----
        # insert certificate key
        # -----END PRIVATE KEY-----
      crt: |
        # -----BEGIN CERTIFICATE-----
        # insert certificate
        # -----END CERTIFICATE-----
    alpn:
      - http/1.1
      - h2
    minversion: "1.1"
    maxversion: "1.3"
    ciphers:
      - TLS_ECDHE_ECDSA_WITH_AES_128_GCM_SHA256
      - TLS_ECDHE_ECDSA_WITH_CHACHA20_POLY1305_SHA256
      - TLS_ECDHE_RSA_WITH_AES_128_GCM_SHA256
      - TLS_ECDHE_RSA_WITH_CHACHA20_POLY1305_SHA256
      - TLS_ECDHE_ECDSA_WITH_AES_256_GCM_SHA384
      - TLS_ECDHE_RSA_WITH_AES_256_GCM_SHA384
```

## TLS Cipher Support on Omni Gateway

Omni can support a range of TLS Versions from TLS 1.2 to TLS 1.3, and you can also customize some of
the ciphers to support.

You cannot customize the list of TLS 1.3 Ciphers. If you support TLS 1.3, the TLS 1.3 default
ciphers are all supported regardless of the ciphers listed in the configuration file. If you donât
support TLS 1.3, these ciphers are not included.

For TLS 1.2, listing any ciphers overides the TLS 1.2 default ciphers. If you want to list ciphers
besides the default TLS 1.2 Ciphers, you must list every cipher to support including the default
ciphers you want to support. Excluding default ciphers from your list of supported ciphers means you
do not support those ciphers. If you wish to only support the default ciphers, you can leave the
cipher list blank. Listing ciphers does not affect the default TLS 1.3 cipher.

> [!IMPORTANT] For outbound TLS Context, ensure that your upstream service supports the selected
> ciphers and versions.

### Omni Gateway Supported Ciphers

Omni Gateway supports the following TLS Ciphers in Connected Mode and Local Mode:

# Transport Layer Security Policy - Inbound

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><tbody><tr><td><p><strong>Policy Name</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Transport Layer Security (TLS) - Inbound</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Summary</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Enables authentication between a client and the API proxy</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Category</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Security</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>First Omni Gateway version available</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>v1.0.0 (inbound mTLS: v1.3.0)</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td rowspan="4"><p><strong>Returned Status Codes</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>No return codes exist for this policy.</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><div></div></td></tr></tbody></table>

> [!NOTE] The following document applies only to Omni Gateway running in Local Mode. To configure
> TLS in Connected Mode, see
> [Configuring TLS Context for Omni Gateway in Connected Mode](flex-conn-tls-config).

## Summary

Omni Gateway supports inbound Transport Layer Security (TLS) and inbound mutual authentication TLS
(mTLS) in Local Mode.

To apply inbound TLS in Local Mode, use the
[Configuring TLS Context for Omni Gateway in Local Mode](flex-local-tls-config) tutorial and refer
to the following configuration files in this documentation.

You can configure an inbound TLS context to enable authentication between a client and the API
instance (HTTPS) by binding the TLS policy to your API instance or to all instances in your Omni
Gateway.

Omni Gateway implements port-level inbound TLS, meaning if you apply an inbound TLS to an API
instance that shares a port with other instances, the same inbound TLS context is applied to all
instances sharing the port. Omni Gateway supports multiple TLS certificates on a single gateway, but
you must apply the certificates to unique ports. If you apply inbound TLS to one API instance, you
do not need to apply the same inbound TLS context to instances that share that port. Consequently,
you cannot apply different inbound TLS contexts to API instances that share the same port.

Port sharing is not a concern for outbound TLS. You can apply an outbound TLS context for each
individual upstream service.

> [!NOTE] TLS policies do not support policy ordering.

## Configuring Policy Parameters

To use HTTPS as your schema when creating an API instance using Omni Gateway as your runtime, you
must manually configure the TLS context in a YAML configuration file.

Refer to the following policy definition and table of parameters:

\- policyRef: name: tls config: requireClientCertificate: <boolean> // OPTIONAL trustedCA: <string>
// OPTIONAL certificate: // OPTIONAL key: <string> // OPTIONAL crt: <string> // OPTIONAL alpn:
<array> // OPTIONAL minversion: <string> // OPTIONAL maxversion: <string> // OPTIONAL ciphers:
<array> // OPTIONAL

Not including optional parameters in your configuration file applies the parameters default values
to your TLS context.

When configuring the `ciphers` parameter, see [TLS Cipher Support on Omni Gateway](#local-support)
for the supported ciphers.

<table><colgroup><col> <col> <col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Parameter</th><th>Required or Optional</th><th>Default Value</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><div><div><p><code>requireClientCertificate</code></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Optional</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>false</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Enables/disables inbound mTLS.</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><div><div><p><code>trustedCA</code></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Optional, unless <code>requireClientCertificate</code> is true</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>N/A</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>The CA used to validate the client certificate. If no trusted CA is provided, the policy uses the default OS CA.</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><div><div><p><code>certificate</code></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Optional</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>N/A</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>A TLS certificate key pair. To comply with security standards, all certificates must be 2048 bits or longer.</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><div><div><p><code>certificate.key</code></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Optional</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>N/A</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>The private key part of the certificate.</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><div><div><p><code>certificate.crt</code></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Optional</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>N/A</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>The public key part of the certificate.</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><div><div><p><code>alpn</code></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Optional</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p><code>h2</code> and <code>http/1.1</code></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>A prioritized list of supported application level protocols; for example, h2, http/1.1, and so forth.</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><div><div><p><code>minversion</code></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Optional</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p><code>1.2</code></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>The minimum TLS version allowed.</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><div><div><p><code>maxversion</code></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Optional</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p><code>1.3</code></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>The maximum TLS version allowed.</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><div><div><p><code>ciphers</code></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Optional</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>For the default and other supported ciphers, see <a href="#local-support">TLS Cipher Support on Omni Gateway</a>.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>A list of supported TLS ciphers (IANA format). For the supported ciphers, see <a href="#local-support">TLS Cipher Support on Omni Gateway</a>.</p></div></div></td></tr></tbody></table>

### Resource Configuration Examples

You can format the YAML file to configure TLS or mTLS either for a specific API instance or for all
API instances running on your Omni Gateway.

#### Apply TLS Configuration to All API Instances

**Sample configuration for adding inbound TLS context for all API instances running on this Omni
Gateway:**

```yaml
apiVersion: gateway.mulesoft.com/v1alpha1
kind: PolicyBinding
metadata:
  name: ingress-https-tls
spec:
  targetRef:
    kind: Selector
    selector:
      kind: ApiInstance
  policyRef:
    name: tls
  config:
    certificate:
      key: |
        # -----BEGIN PRIVATE KEY-----
        # insert certificate key
        # -----END PRIVATE KEY-----
      crt: |
        # -----BEGIN CERTIFICATE-----
        # insert certificate
        # -----END CERTIFICATE-----
    alpn:
      - http/1.1
      - h2
    minversion: "1.1"
    maxversion: "1.3"
    ciphers:
      - TLS_ECDHE_ECDSA_WITH_AES_128_GCM_SHA256
      - TLS_CHACHA20_POLY1305_SHA256
      - TLS_ECDHE_RSA_WITH_AES_128_GCM_SHA256
      - TLS_ECDHE_RSA_WITH_CHACHA20_POLY1305_SHA256
      - TLS_ECDHE_ECDSA_WITH_AES_128_CBC_SHA
      - TLS_ECDHE_RSA_WITH_AES_128_CBC_SHA
      - TLS_RSA_WITH_AES_128_GCM_SHA256
      - TLS_RSA_WITH_AES_128_CBC_SHA
      - TLS_ECDHE_ECDSA_WITH_AES_256_GCM_SHA384
      - TLS_ECDHE_RSA_WITH_AES_256_GCM_SHA384
      - TLS_ECDHE_ECDSA_WITH_AES_256_CBC_SHA
      - TLS_ECDHE_RSA_WITH_AES_256_CBC_SHA
      - TLS_RSA_WITH_AES_256_GCM_SHA384
      - TLS_RSA_WITH_AES_256_CBC_SHA
```

#### Apply TLS Configuration to a Specific API Instance

**Sample configuration for adding inbound TLS context for a specific API instance:**

```yaml
apiVersion: gateway.mulesoft.com/v1alpha1
kind: PolicyBinding
metadata:
  name: ingress-https-tls
spec:
  targetRef:
    kind: ApiInstance
    name: ingress-https
  policyRef:
    name: tls
  config:
    certificate:
      key: |
        # -----BEGIN PRIVATE KEY-----
        # insert certificate key
        # -----END PRIVATE KEY-----
      crt: |
        # -----BEGIN CERTIFICATE-----
        # insert certificate
        # -----END CERTIFICATE-----
    alpn:
      - http/1.1
      - h2
    minversion: "1.1"
    maxversion: "1.3"
    ciphers:
      - TLS_ECDHE_ECDSA_WITH_AES_128_GCM_SHA256
      - TLS_CHACHA20_POLY1305_SHA256
      - TLS_ECDHE_RSA_WITH_AES_128_GCM_SHA256
      - TLS_ECDHE_RSA_WITH_CHACHA20_POLY1305_SHA256
      - TLS_ECDHE_ECDSA_WITH_AES_128_CBC_SHA
      - TLS_ECDHE_RSA_WITH_AES_128_CBC_SHA
      - TLS_RSA_WITH_AES_128_GCM_SHA256
      - TLS_RSA_WITH_AES_128_CBC_SHA
      - TLS_ECDHE_ECDSA_WITH_AES_256_GCM_SHA384
      - TLS_ECDHE_RSA_WITH_AES_256_GCM_SHA384
      - TLS_ECDHE_ECDSA_WITH_AES_256_CBC_SHA
      - TLS_ECDHE_RSA_WITH_AES_256_CBC_SHA
      - TLS_RSA_WITH_AES_256_GCM_SHA384
      - TLS_RSA_WITH_AES_256_CBC_SHA
```

#### Apply mTLS Inbound Configuration to a Specific API Instance

**Sample configuration for adding an inbound mTLS context for a specific API instance:**

```yaml
apiVersion: gateway.mulesoft.com/v1alpha1
kind: PolicyBinding
metadata:
  name: mtls
spec:
  targetRef:
    name: ingress-https
  policyRef:
    name: tls
  config:
    requireClientCertificate: true
    trustedCA: |
      # -----BEGIN CERTIFICATE-----
      # insert certificate
      # -----END CERTIFICATE-----
    certificate:
      key: |
        # -----BEGIN RSA PRIVATE KEY-----
        # insert private key
        # -----END RSA PRIVATE KEY-----
      crt: |
        # -----BEGIN CERTIFICATE-----
        # insert certificate
        # -----END CERTIFICATE-----
```

This example uses the default values for `alpn`, `minversion`, `maxversion`, and `ciphers`.

## TLS Cipher Support on Omni Gateway

Omni can support a range of TLS Versions from TLS 1.2 to TLS 1.3, and you can also customize some of
the ciphers to support.

You cannot customize the list of TLS 1.3 Ciphers. If you support TLS 1.3, the TLS 1.3 default
ciphers are all supported regardless of the ciphers listed in the configuration file. If you donât
support TLS 1.3, these ciphers are not included.

For TLS 1.2, listing any ciphers overides the TLS 1.2 default ciphers. If you want to list ciphers
besides the default TLS 1.2 Ciphers, you must list every cipher to support including the default
ciphers you want to support. Excluding default ciphers from your list of supported ciphers means you
do not support those ciphers. If you wish to only support the default ciphers, you can leave the
cipher list blank. Listing ciphers does not affect the default TLS 1.3 cipher.

### Omni Gateway Supported Ciphers

Omni Gateway supports the following TLS Ciphers in Connected Mode and Local Mode:

<table><colgroup><col> <col> <col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Cipher</th><th>TLS Version</th><th>Default</th><th>Advice</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><div><div><p>TLS_AES_128_GCM_SHA256</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>1.3</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Yes</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Secure</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><div><div><p>TLS_AES_256_GCM_SHA384</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>1.3</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Yes</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Secure</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><div><div><p>TLS_CHACHA20_POLY1305_SHA256</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>1.3</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Yes</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Secure</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><div><div><p>TLS_ECDHE_ECDSA_WITH_AES_128_GCM_SHA256</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>1.2</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Yes</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Recommended</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><div><div><p>TLS_ECDHE_ECDSA_WITH_AES_256_GCM_SHA384</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>1.2</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Yes</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Recommended</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><div><div><p>TLS_ECDHE_ECDSA_WITH_CHACHA20_POLY1305_SHA256</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>1.2</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Yes</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Recommended</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><div><div><p>TLS_ECDHE_PSK_WITH_CHACHA20_POLY1305_SHA256</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>1.2</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>No</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Recommended</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><div><div><p>TLS_ECDHE_RSA_WITH_AES_128_GCM_SHA256</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>1.2</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Yes</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Secure</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><div><div><p>TLS_ECDHE_RSA_WITH_AES_256_GCM_SHA384</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>1.2</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Yes</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Secure</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><div><div><p>TLS_ECDHE_RSA_WITH_CHACHA20_POLY1305_SHA256</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>1.2</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Yes</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Secure</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><div><div><p>TLS_RSA_WITH_AES_128_GCM_SHA256</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>1.2</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>No</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Weak</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><div><div><p>TLS_RSA_WITH_AES_256_GCM_SHA384</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>1.2</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>No</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Weak</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><div><div><p>TLS_RSA_WITH_AES_128_CBC_SHA</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>1.2</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>No</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Weak</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><div><div><p>TLS_RSA_WITH_AES_256_CBC_SHA</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>1.2</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>No</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Weak</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><div><div><p>TLS_PSK_WITH_AES_128_CBC_SHA</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>1.2</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>No</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Weak</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><div><div><p>TLS_PSK_WITH_AES_256_CBC_SHA</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>1.2</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>No</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Weak</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><div><div><p>TLS_ECDHE_ECDSA_WITH_AES_128_CBC_SHA</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>1.2</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>No</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Weak</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><div><div><p>TLS_ECDHE_ECDSA_WITH_AES_256_CBC_SHA</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>1.2</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>No</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Weak</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><div><div><p>TLS_ECDHE_RSA_WITH_AES_128_CBC_SHA</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>1.2</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>No</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Weak</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><div><div><p>TL # Tracing Policy

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><tbody><tr><td><p><strong>Policy Name</strong></p></td><td><p>Tracing</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Summary</strong></p></td><td><p>Override global tracing configuration</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Category</strong></p></td><td><p>Troubleshooting</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>First Omni Gateway version available</strong></p></td><td><p>1.10.0</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Returned Status Codes</strong></p></td><td><p>No return codes exist for this policy</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

## Summary

The Tracing policy enables you to override the global distributed tracing parameters for an API.

## Before You Begin

Distributed tracing is disabled by default. After you have enabled distributed tracing with an Omni
Gateway global configuration, you can use this policy to override the global distributed tracing
parameters for an API.

For details on how to enable distributed tracing, see:

- [Configuring Omni Gateway Distributed Tracing in Local Mode](flex-local-tracing-config)
- [Configuring Omni Gateway Distributed Tracing in Connected Mode](flex-conn-tracing-config)
- [Configuring Distributed Tracing for Managed Omni Gateway](flex-managed-tracing-config)

## Configuring Policy Parameters

### Omni Gateway Local Mode

When you apply the policy via declarative configuration files, Refer to the following policy
definition and table of parameters:

\- policyRef: name: tracing-flex config: sampling: client: number // REQUIRED random: number //
REQUIRED overall: number // REQUIRED spanName: <string> // OPTIONAL labels: <array> // OPTIONAL,
default: \[\] - name: <string> // REQUIRED type: <string> // REQUIRED keyName: <string> // REQUIRED
defaultValue: <string> // REQUIRED

<table><colgroup><col> <col> <col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Parameter</th><th>Required or Optional</th><th>Default Value</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><div><div><p><code>sampling.client</code></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Required</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>100</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>The target percentage of requests that are force traced if the <code>x-client-trace-id</code> header is set.</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><div><div><p><code>sampling.random</code></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Required</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>100</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>The target percentage of requests that are randomly selected for trace generation, if not requested by the client or not forced.</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><div><div><p><code>sampling.overall</code></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Required</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>100</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>The target percentage of requests that are traced after all other sampling checks have been applied, such as client-directed, force tracing, and random sampling.</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><div><div><p><code>spanName</code></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Optional</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>N/A</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>The name of the span. If no span name is configured, the API name is used.</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><div><div><p><code>labels</code></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Optional</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Empty array</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Array of labels that are added to the trace to be used as span attributes in the trace data.</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><div><div><p><code>labels.name</code></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Required</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>N/A</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>The name of the label, used as a span attribute name.</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><div><div><p><code>labels.type</code></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Required</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>N/A</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>The type of the label specifies where the value for the label is taken from. Supported values are <code>literal</code>, <code>environment</code>, and <code>requestHeader</code>.</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><div><div><p><code>labels.keyName</code></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Required</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>N/A</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>The key name of the label.</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><div><div><p><code>labels.defaultValue</code></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Required</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>N/A</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>The default value of the label.</p></div></div></td></tr></tbody></table>

#### Resource Configuration Example

\- policyRef: name: tracing-flex config: sampling: client: 100 random: 100 overall: 100 spanName:
flightsAPI labels: - name: c-env-tag type: environment keyName: FLEX_NAME defaultValue:
DEFAULT_VAL - name: c-header-tag type: requestHeader keyName: :method defaultValue: DEFAULT_METHOD -
name: c-literal-tag type: literal defaultValue: the-tag

### Managed Omni Gateway and Omni Gateway Connected Mode

When you apply the policy from the UI, the following parameters are displayed:

<table><colgroup><col> <col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Element</th><th>Description</th><th>Required</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><div><div><p>Client Sampling</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>The target percentage of requests that are force traced if the <code>x-client-trace-id</code> header is set.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Yes</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><div><div><p>Random Sampling</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>The target percentage of requests that are randomly selected for trace generation, if not requested by the client or not forced.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Yes</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><div><div><p>Overall Sampling</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>The target percentage of requests that are traced after all other sampling checks have been applied, such as client-directed, force tracing, and random sampling.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Yes</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><div><div><p>Span name</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>The name of the span. If no span name is configured, the API name is used.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>No</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><div><div><p>Labels</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Array of labels that are added to the trace to be used as span attributes in the trace data.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Optional</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><div><div><p>Label Name</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>The name of the label, used as a span attribute name.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Yes</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><div><div><p>Label Type</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>The type of the label specifies where the value for the label is taken from. Supported values are <code>literal</code>, <code>environment</code>, and <code>requestHeader</code>.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Yes</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><div><div><p>Label Key Name</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>The key used to retrieve the value, such as an environment variable or request header name.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Yes</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><div><div><p>Label Default Value</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Default value to use if the key is not found or the retrieved value is empty.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Yes</p></div></div></td></tr></tbody></table>
---

# Traffic Management for Multiple Upstream Services

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><tbody><tr><td><p><strong>Policy Name</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Traffic Management for Multiple Upstream Services</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Summary</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Manages API instance traffic to multiple upstream services from a single consumer endpoint</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>First Omni Gateway version available</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>v1.0.0</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td rowspan="4"><p><strong>Returned Status Codes</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>No return codes exist for this policy.</p></div></div></td></tr></tbody></table>

> [!NOTE] The following information applies only to Omni Gateway running in Local Mode. To configure
> multiple upstream services for Omni Gateway running in Connected Mode, see
> [Multiple Upstream Services for Omni Gateway Running in Connected Mode](../../api-manager/latest/create-instance-task-flex#traffic-management).

## Summary

Omni Gateway manages request traffic by using different routes that can each direct traffic to
multiple upstream services. Omni Gateway directs traffic to the routes by using the route order and
the individual routeâs rules. Additionally, you can add a weighted percentage to each upstream
service within a route to manage the percentage of requests sent to the upstream service. For
information about adding weight percentages, refer to
[Traffic Management for Multiple Upstream Services (Weighted)](policies-included-traffic-management).

To allow routing requests to a `Service` when an `ApiInstance` does not include `spec.services`, you
must define a `PolicyBinding` configuration with the Traffic Management for Multiple Upstream
Services (`route`) policy. You donât need to configure a `route` policy if a custom policy is
managing requests.

## Configuring Policy Parameters

Refer to the following policy definition and table of parameters:

policyRef: name: route config: destinationPath: <string> // OPTIONAL, default: "/" destinationRef:
name: <string> // REQUIRED kind: <string> // OPTIONAL, default: "Service" namespace: <string> //
OPTIONAL, default: "" rules:

- path: <URL-regex> // OPTIONAL, Example: (.\*) methods: <regex-string> // OPTIONAL, Example:
  GET|POST headers: <header-name>: <regex-string> // OPTIONAL host: <regex-string> // OPTIONAL

<table><colgroup><col> <col> <col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Parameter</th><th>Required or Optional</th><th>Default Value</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><div><div><p><code>destinationPath</code></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Optional</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>"/"</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Path of the upstream service</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><div><div><p><code>destinationRef.name</code></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Required</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>N/A</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Name of the upstream service as defined in the <code>Service</code> configuration resource</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><div><div><p><code>destinationRef.kind</code></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Optional</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>"Service"</p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><div><div><p><code>destinationRef.namespace</code></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Optional</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>""</p></div></div></td><td><div></div></td></tr><tr><td><div><div><p><code>rules</code></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Optional</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Empty</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Array of rulesets for this route.<br>Each route can support multiple rulesets.</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><div><div><p><code>rules.path</code></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Optional</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Empty</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Request path that the route can service.<br>You can define only one âURI Template Regex" path for each ruleset. Only requests with the defined path are sent to this route.</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><div><div><p><code>rules.methods</code></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Optional</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Empty</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Array that defines the types of request methods that the route can service.<br>You can select multiple methods for each ruleset. Only requests that are one of the defined methods are sent to this route. Supported values are <code>GET</code>, <code>POST</code>, <code>PUT</code>, <code>PATCH</code>, <code>DELETE</code>, <code>OPTIONS</code>, <code>HEAD</code>, <code>TRACE</code>, and <code>CONNECT</code>.</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><div><div><p><code>rules.headers</code></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Optional</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Empty</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Array that defines what headers and regular expression value must be present for this route to service the request.<br>Only requests that meet all of the specified header requirements are sent to this route. Additional headers present in the request that are not specifically defined in the rules are ignored.</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><div><div><p><code>rules.host</code></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Optional</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Empty</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Request host URL that the route can service.<br>You can define only one host URL for each ruleset. Only requests made from the defined host are sent to this route.</p></div></div></td></tr></tbody></table>

### Resource Configuration Example

You can define an `ApiInstance` configuration that does not include the `spec.services` parameter:

```yaml
apiVersion: gateway.mulesoft.com/v1alpha1
kind: ApiInstance
metadata:
  name: ingress-http
spec:
  address: http://0.0.0.0:8080/api
```

Given the absence of the `spec.services` parameter, you must define the `route` policy in a
`PolicyBinding` configuration:

```yaml
apiVersion: gateway.mulesoft.com/v1alpha1
kind: PolicyBinding
metadata:
  name: ingress-httpbin-route
spec:
  targetRef:
    name: ingress-http
  policyRef:
    name: route
  config:
    destinationPath: /orders
    destinationRef:
      name: orders-svc
  rules:
    - path: /foo(/.*)
```

The related `Service` configuration:

```yaml
apiVersion: gateway.mulesoft.com/v1alpha1
kind: Service
metadata:
  name: orders-svc
spec:
  address: https://orders.svc.local:443/
```

## Determining a Request Path

The following table describes all the possible parameter combinations, with examples of the
attributes that determine routing and the expected transformation of a requestâs path. The columns
refer to data values of the attributes in the previous configuration examples.

<table><colgroup><col> <col> <col> <col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>ApiInstance .address .path</th><th>PolicyBinding(route) .rules .path</th><th>PolicyBinding(route) .config .destinationPath</th><th>Input Path</th><th>Output Path</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p>&lt;empty&gt;</p></td><td><p>/api/.*</p></td><td><p>&lt;empty&gt;</p></td><td><p>/api/foo/bar</p></td><td><p>/api/foo/bar</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>&lt;empty&gt;</p></td><td><p>/api(/.*)</p></td><td><p>&lt;empty&gt;</p></td><td><p>/api/foo/bar</p></td><td><p>/foo/bar</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>&lt;empty&gt;</p></td><td><p>/api/.*</p></td><td><p>/orders</p></td><td><p>/api/foo/bar</p></td><td><p>/orders/api/foo/bar</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>&lt;empty&gt;</p></td><td><p>/api(/.*)</p></td><td><p>/orders</p></td><td><p>/api/foo/bar</p></td><td><p>/orders/foo/bar</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>/api</p></td><td><p>/foo/.*</p></td><td><p>&lt;empty&gt;</p></td><td><p>/api/foo/bar</p></td><td><p>/foo/bar</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>/api</p></td><td><p>/foo(/.*)</p></td><td><p>&lt;empty&gt;</p></td><td><p>/api/foo/bar</p></td><td><p>/bar</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>/api</p></td><td><p>/foo/.*</p></td><td><p>/orders</p></td><td><p>/api/foo/bar</p></td><td><p>/orders/foo/bar</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>/api</p></td><td><p>/foo(/.*)</p></td><td><p>/orders</p></td><td><p>/api/foo/bar</p></td><td><p>/orders/bar</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>/api</p></td><td><p>/api/foo/.*</p></td><td><p>/orders</p></td><td><p>/api/foo/bar</p></td><td><p>&lt;no match&gt; (expected a path like /api/api/foo/bar)</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>/api</p></td><td><p>/foo(/.)/bar(/.)</p></td><td><p>&lt;empty&gt;</p></td><td><p>/api/foo/abc/bar/def</p></td><td><p>/abc/def</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>/api</p></td><td><p>/foo(/.)/bar(/.)</p></td><td><p>/orders</p></td><td><p>/api/foo/abc/bar/def</p></td><td><p>/orders/abc/def</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

# Traffic Management for Multiple Upstream Services (Weighted)

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><tbody><tr><td><p><strong>Policy Name</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Traffic Management for Multiple Upstream Services (Weighted)</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Summary</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Manages weighted API instance traffic to multiple upstream services from a single consumer endpoint</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>First Omni Gateway version available</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>v1.5.0</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td rowspan="4"><p><strong>Returned Status Codes</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>No return codes exist for this policy.</p></div></div></td></tr></tbody></table>

> [!NOTE] The following information applies only to Omni Gateway running in Local Mode. To configure
> multiple upstream services for Omni Gateway running in Connected Mode, see
> [Multiple Upstream Services for Omni Gateway Running in Connected Mode](../../api-manager/latest/create-instance-task-flex#traffic-management).

## Summary

Omni Gateway running in Local Mode supports API instances that expose multiple upstream services
through a single consumer endpoint.

Omni Gateway manages request traffic by using different routes that can each direct traffic to
multiple upstream services. Omni Gateway directs traffic to the routes by using the route order and
the individual routeâs rules. Additionally, you can add a weighted percentage to each upstream
service within a route to manage the percentage of requests sent to the upstream service. For
information about configuring non-weighted traffic, refer to
[Traffic Management for Multiple Upstream Services](policies-included-traffic-management-route).

API instances with multiple upstream services in Local Mode are configured by using `PolicyBinding`
YAML configuration resources. Each `route-weighted` resource defines a single route. To add
additional routes, add multiple `route-weighted` resources.

For a tutorial about how to publish an API instance with this policy applied, see
[Publishing an API Instance with Multiple Upstream Services in Local Mode](flex-local-publish-api-multiple-services).

> [!NOTE] Deploying APIs with multiple upstream services does not affect the
> [Outbound Transport Layer Security policy](policies-included-tls-outbound). You can apply an
> Outbound TLS policy to each upstream service.

## Configuring Policy Parameters

Refer to the following policy definition and table of parameters:

policyRef: name: route-weighted config: routes:

- weight: <int> // REQUIRED, max_value=100, min_value=1 destinationPath: <string> // OPTIONAL,
  default: "/" destinationRef: name: <string> // REQUIRED rules:
- path: <URL-regex> // OPTIONAL, Example: (.\*) methods: <regex-string> // OPTIONAL, Example:
  GET|POST headers: <header-name>: <regex-string> // OPTIONAL host: <regex-string> // OPTIONAL

<table><colgroup><col> <col> <col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Parameter</th><th>Required or Optional</th><th>Default Value</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><div><div><p><code>routes</code></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Required</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Empty</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Array of API upstream services.<br>At least one upstream is required.</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><div><div><p><code>routes.weight</code></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Required</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>N/A</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Weight of total requests sent to this upstream service.<br>The percentage of traffic sent to that upstream service is the upstream serviceâs weight divided by total weight of all upstream services.</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><div><div><p><code>routes.destinationPath</code></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Required</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>N/A</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Path of the upstream service</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><div><div><p><code>routes.destinationRef.name</code></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Required</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>N/A</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Name of the upstream service as defined in the <code>Service</code> configuration resource</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><div><div><p><code>rules</code></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Optional</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Empty</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Array of rulesets for this route.<br>Each route can support multiple rulesets.</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><div><div><p><code>rules.path</code></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Optional</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Empty</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Request path that the route can service.<br>You can define only one âURI Template Regex" path for each ruleset. Only requests with the defined path are sent to this route.</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><div><div><p><code>rules.methods</code></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Optional</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Empty</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Array that defines the types of request methods that the route can service.<br>You can select multiple methods for each ruleset. Only requests that are one of the defined methods are sent to this route. Supported values are <code>GET</code>, <code>POST</code>, <code>PUT</code>, <code>PATCH</code>, <code>DELETE</code>, <code>OPTIONS</code>, <code>HEAD</code>, <code>TRACE</code>, and <code>CONNECT</code>.</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><div><div><p><code>rules.headers</code></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Optional</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Empty</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Array that defines what headers and regular expression value must be present for this route to service the request.<br>Only requests that meet all of the specified header requirements are sent to this route. Additional headers present in the request that are not specifically defined in the rules are ignored.</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><div><div><p><code>rules.host</code></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Optional</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Empty</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Request host URL that the route can service.<br>You can define only one host URL for each ruleset. Only requests made from the defined host are sent to this route.</p></div></div></td></tr></tbody></table>

## How This Policy Works

The Traffic Management policy directs traffic to different sets of upstream services by using
multiple routes with a defined route order and individual rulesets to direct traffic to different
sets of upstream services.

In the following diagram, different routes manage requests to flight information databases and to a
customer service application. Route one has two upstream services defined, which directs 70% of
requests to a stable database and 30% of requests to a beta database.

![Omni Gateway manages the traffic to multiple upstream services](_images/multiple-upstreams.png)

To achieve this configuration, bind two `route-weighted` resources to the exposed API instance. One
`route-weighted` resource directs traffic to the flight information database, and the other
`route-weighted` resource directs traffic to the customer service application.

### Upstream Services

You can use multiple upstream services in a single route to direct requests to similar services. For
example, to test the performance of a new beta upstream service without sending all traffic to the
new service, you can direct half of the traffic to a stable upstream service and half to the new
upstream service.

The upstream service within the route that each request is sent to is random and independent of any
previous request. The `routes.weight` value defines the chance of a request being sent to a
particular upstream service. The percentage of traffic sent to that upstream service is the
upstreamâs `routes.weight` divided by the total weight of all upstream services. For example, if
there are two upstream services with weight `1`, each receives 50% of the traffic to that route. If
the weight values are `1` and `3`, one route would receive 25% of traffic and the other 75%.

Because any upstream service within a route can receive any request, all upstream services within
the same route must adhere to the same API contract.

### Route Rules

You can direct requests to different routes by using route rules. Requests must meet the rules for
only one of the rulesets in the route.

Rulesets refer to any combination of the four rules: `path`, `method`, `headers`, and `host`. You
can use multiple rulesets to support different combinations of rules.

All rules are optional. If a rule is not included in a ruleset, that rule is ignored. For example,
not specifying a host means that the route can service any host if the request meets the other route
rules. Not defining any rule means that the route can service every request.

If a request does not meet the rules for any route, Omni Gateway returns a `404` error code.

### Route Order

In addition to using route rules, you can direct requests to different routes by using policy
ordering.

To order policies, see [Ordering Policies](policies-reorder)

Setting the policy order sets the order in which traffic is directed to the routes. Omni Gateway
directs requests to the first route if the request meets one of the route rulesets. Therefore, route
ordering is very important when a request can meet the route rules of multiple routes.

For example, in a configuration in which route one has the `GET` method defined as a rule and route
two has no route rules defined, all `GET` requests are sent to route one and all other requests are
sent to route two. If the route order was reversed and route one had no route rules, Omni Gateway
would direct all requests to route one before any `GET` requests could reach route two.

## See Also

- [Publishing an API Instance with Multiple Upstream Services in Local Mode](flex-local-publish-api-multiple-services)
---

# WebSocket Connection Limit Policy

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><tbody><tr><td><p><strong>Policy Name</strong></p></td><td><p>WebSocket Connection Limit</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Summary</strong></p></td><td><p>Limits the number of simultaneous WebSocket connections that each API instance allows</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Category</strong></p></td><td><p>Quality of Service</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>First Omni Gateway version available</strong></p></td><td><p>v1.13.0</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Returned status codes</strong></p></td><td><p>429 - <code>Too many WebSocket connections</code></p></td></tr></tbody></table>

## Summary

The WebSocket Connection Limit policy tracks the number of active WebSocket connections for a
specific API instance. Omni Gateway identifies these requests by the `Upgrade: websocket` header and
ignores standard HTTP traffic. The WebSocket connection limit is distributed across Omni replicas.

When a WebSocket upgrade request arrives, Omni Gateway increments the counter to reserve capacity
before forwarding the handshake to the upstream service. If the upstream service doesnât return a
`101 Switching Protocols` response, Omni Gateway releases the reservation. After a successful
WebSocket connection closes, Omni Gateway decrements the counter.

If the counter reaches the `maximumConnections` limit, Omni Gateway rejects the upgrade request with
a `429` error code.

## Configuring Policy Parameters

### Omni Gateway Local Mode

When you apply the policy via declarative configuration files, Refer to the following policy
definition and table of parameters:

\- policyRef: name: websocket-connection-limit-flex config: maximumConnections: <integer> //
REQUIRED, minimum 1

<table><colgroup><col> <col> <col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Parameter</th><th>Required</th><th>Default Value</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><div><div><p><code>maximumConnections</code></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Yes</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p><code>100</code></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Maximum number of simultaneous WebSocket connections allowed for the API instance. The value must be an integer of 1 or greater.</p></div></div></td></tr></tbody></table>

#### Resource Configuration Example

```yaml
- policyRef:
    name: websocket-connection-limit-flex
  config:
    maximumConnections: 500
```

### Managed Omni Gateway and Omni Gateway Connected Mode

When you apply the policy from the UI, the following parameters are displayed:

<table><colgroup><col> <col> <col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Field</th><th>Description</th><th>Default</th><th>Required</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><div><div><p>Maximum Connections</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Maximum number of simultaneous WebSocket connections allowed for the API instance.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p><code>100</code></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Yes</p></div></div></td></tr></tbody></table>

## See Also

- [Inbound Policies Directory](policies-included-directory)
- [Securing Omni Gateway Instances with Policies](flex-gateway-secure-apis)

# XML Threat Protection Policy

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><tbody><tr><td><p><strong>Policy Name</strong></p></td><td><p>XML Threat Protection</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Summary</strong></p></td><td><p>Protects against malicious XML in API requests</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Category</strong></p></td><td><p>Security</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>First Omni Gateway version available</strong></p></td><td><p>v1.10.0</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Returned Status Codes</strong></p></td><td><p>400 - Bad Request</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

## Summary

Applications that process XML requests are susceptible to attacks characterized by unusual inflation
of elements, attributes, and deep nesting levels. Attackers use recursive techniques to consume
memory resources. Dramatic increases in the size of the application data often signal a security
risk. Use the XML Threat Protection policy to reject unusual requests that exceed the defined
expected size.

## Configuring Policy Parameters

### Omni Gateway Local Mode

When you apply the policy via declarative configuration files, Refer to the following policy
definition and table of parameters:

\- policyRef: name: xml-threat-protection-flex config: maxNodeDepth: <number> // OPTIONAL, default:
-1 maxAttributeCountPerElement: <number> // OPTIONAL, default: -1 maxChildCount: <number> //
OPTIONAL, default: -1 maxTextLength: <number> // OPTIONAL, default: -1 maxAttributeLength: <number>
// OPTIONAL, default: -1 maxCommentLength: <number> // OPTIONAL, default: -1

> [!NOTE] Defining a parameter as `-1` means the parameter has no limit.

<table><colgroup><col> <col> <col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Parameter</th><th>Required</th><th>Default Value</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><div><div><p><code>maxNodeDepth</code></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>No</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>-1</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Specifies the maximum node depth of an XML document.</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><div><div><p><code>maxAttributeCountPerElement</code></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>No</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>-1</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Specifies the maximum number of attributes in an element. Attributes used for defining namespaces are not counted.</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><div><div><p><code>maxChildCount</code></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>No</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>-1</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Specifies the maximum number of children of an element in the XML document.</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><div><div><p><code>maxTextLength</code></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>No</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>-1</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Specifies the maximum length in characters of text nodes in the XML document.</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><div><div><p><code>maxAttributeLength</code></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>No</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>-1</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Specifies the maximum length in characters of an attribute in the XML document.</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><div><div><p><code>maxCommentLength</code></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>No</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>-1</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Specifies the maximum number of comment characters in the XML document.</p></div></div></td></tr></tbody></table>

#### Resource Configuration Example

\- policyRef: name: xml-threat-protection-flex config: maxNodeDepth: 1 maxAttributeCountPerElement:
1 maxChildCount: 2 maxTextLength: 20 maxAttributeLength: 20 maxCommentLength: 20

### Managed Omni Gateway and Omni Gateway Connected Mode

When you apply the policy from the UI, the following parameters are displayed:

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Field</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><div><div><p>Maximum Node Depth</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Specifies the maximum node depth of an XML document.</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><div><div><p>Maximum Attribute Count Per Element</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Specifies the maximum number of attributes in an element. Attributes used for defining namespaces are not counted.</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><div><div><p>Maximum Child Count</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Specifies the maximum number of children of an element in the XML document.</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><div><div><p>Maximum Text Length</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Specifies the maximum length in characters of text nodes in the XML document.</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><div><div><p>Maximum Attribute Length</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Specifies the maximum length in characters of an attribute in the XML document.</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><div><div><p>Maximum Comment Length</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Specifies the maximum number of comment characters in the XML document.</p></div></div></td></tr></tbody></table>
---

# Configuring MCP Access Control Policies

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

Omni Gateway provides three MCP policies that work together to control which tools and related MCP
entities are exposed:

- [MCP Global Access](policies-included-mcp-global-access): Restricts which tools are exposed by
  using allow or block rules.
- [MCP Tool Mapping](policies-included-mcp-tool-mapping): Redefines tool names and descriptions to
  reduce prompt complexity and improve LLM comprehension.
- [MCP Attribute-Based Access Control](policies-included-mcp-attribute-access-control): Grants each
  client a subset of tools based on who they are, such as Tiers, IP, headers, or claims.

## How the Policies Work Together

You can use MCP policies individually or combine them to fit your use cases. When you use multiple
policies together, stack them in this order from the gateway toward the client so that they work as
intended:

MCP Global Access

Filters the overall tool list. Define rules to allow or block specific tools so that only the tools
you want to expose enter your network.

MCP Tool Mapping

Redefines tool names and descriptions to reduce prompt complexity and improve LLM comprehension.
Mapping doesnât filter tools. If you use both policies, MCP Global Access must allow a tool before
mapping applies.

MCP Attribute-Based Access Control

Grants each connecting client a tailored subset of the available tools based on attributes, such as
tiers, IP addresses, headers, or claims.

To configure the policy order, see [Ordering Policies](policies-reorder).

## See Also

- [MCP Global Access](policies-included-mcp-global-access)
