# A2A In-Task Authorization Code Policy

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><tbody><tr><td><p><strong>Policy Name</strong></p></td><td><p>A2A In-Task Authorization Code</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Summary</strong></p></td><td><p>Ensures secondary credentials are present for in-task authentication using OAuth 2.0 Authorization Code flow</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Category</strong></p></td><td><p>A2A</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>First Omni Gateway version available</strong></p></td><td><p>v1.11.4</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Returned Status Codes</strong></p></td><td><p>200 (authentication challenge), 401 (authentication failure)</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

> [!NOTE] This policy supports Agent2Agent Protocol (A2A) version v0.3.0. To learn more about A2A
> versions, see [A2A Releases](https://github.com/a2aproject/A2A/releases).

## Summary

The In-Task Authorization Code policy is an outbound policy that ensures secondary credentials are
present for in-task authentication using OAuth 2.0 Authorization Code flow. When requests require
additional authentication beyond the initial connection, this policy:

1.  Detects if a request contains a secondary authentication token at
    `$.params.message.parts[].data.auth_credentials.accessToken`. This policy assumes that all
    requests besides agent card requests require secondary authentication.
2.  If a secondary token is missing, the policy returns an `auth-required` challenge with details of
    where the agent can obtain the token from the OAuth 2.0 authentication provider.

    To learn more about the authentication challenge response, see
    [Challenge Response Format](#challenge-response).

3.  If the token was initially provided or returned from the challenge response, the policy extracts
    the token from the request body.
4.  The policy sets the extracted token in the `Authorization` header and removes the token from the
    request body to prevent token leakage.

    To learn more about token handling, see [Token Handling](#token-handling).

5.  The policy then forwards the request to upstream services.

When the inbound user can be identified, the policy reuses secondary tokens from a token cache. To
learn more about how this policy caches requests, see [Token caching](#token-caching-intask).

> [!NOTE] To ensure agent discovery works uninterrupted, requests to these agent card endpoints
> bypass the policy without authentication:
>
> - `/.well-known/agent-card.json`
> - Any path ending in `agent-card.json`

## Configuring Policy Parameters

### Omni Gateway Local Mode

The In-Task Authorization Code policy isnât supported in Local Mode.

### Managed Omni Gateway and Omni Gateway Connected Mode

When you apply the policy from the UI, the following parameters are displayed:

<table><colgroup><col> <col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Element</th><th>Description</th><th>Required</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><div><div><p>Secondary Auth Provider</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Name of the OAuth 2.0 identity provider. For example: <code>okta</code>, <code>azure-ad</code>, <code>auth0</code>.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Optional</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><div><div><p>Authorization Endpoint</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Authorization endpoint URL of the OAuth 2.0 service. For example: <code>https://dev-12345.okta.com/oauth2/v1/authorize</code>.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Required</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><div><div><p>Token Endpoint</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Token endpoint of the OAuth 2.0 service. For example: <code>https://dev-12345.okta.com/oauth2/v1/token</code>.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Required</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><div><div><p>Scopes</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Space or comma separated list of the OAuth 2.0 scopes required for in-task authentication. For example: <code>openid profile email transaction:execute</code>.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Required</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><div><div><p>Redirect URI</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>OAuth 2.0 redirect URI that the client uses in the authorization flow.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Required</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><div><div><p>Token Audience</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>OAuth 2.0 token audience. The intended recipient of the token. For example: <code>https://my-agent.example.com/api</code>.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Optional</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><div><div><p>Response Type</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>OAuth 2.0 response type. Typically <code>code</code> for authorization code flow.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Optional</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><div><div><p>Code Challenge Method</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>PKCE code challenge method. Typically <code>S256</code> for SHA-256.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Optional</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><div><div><p>Body Encoding</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Encoding format for token request body. Typically <code>form</code> for <code>application/x-www-form-urlencoded</code>. Default: <code>form</code>.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Optional</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><div><div><p>Token Timeout</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Timeout in seconds for token requests.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Optional</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><div><div><p>Challenge Response Status Code</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>HTTP status code to return for <code>auth-required</code> challenge responses. Typically <code>200</code> to maintain JSON-RPC compatibility.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Optional</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><div><div><p>Distributed</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>When enabled, the token cache is shared across Omni Gateway replicas. Enable this option when running more than one replica to avoid duplicate token requests or repeated auth challenges.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Optional</p></div></div></td></tr></tbody></table>

## Token Handling

For `GET` and `POST` requests, the policy handles the token differently:

- **GET**: The policy removes the request body after the token extraction.
- **POST**: The policy removes only the token property from the body and preserves the rest of the
  body.

The token is always removed from the body after extraction to prevent token leakage to the upstream
service. The extracted token is set in the `Authorization` header as the `Bearer <token>` before
forwarding the request. This overrides any existing `Authorization` header. Perform any
authentication of the first authorization header prior to this policy.

The policy extracts the token from `$.params.message.parts[].data.auth_credentials.accessToken`. For
example, using the default JSONPath, the policy extracts the token
(`"eyJhbGciOiJSUzI1NiIsInR5cCI6IkpXVCJ9â¦â"`) from the following request body:

```json
{
  "jsonrpc": "2.0",
  "id": "req-18",
  "method": "message/send",
  "params": {
    "taskId": "task-123",
    "contextId": "ctx-456",
    "message": {
      "role": "user",
      "parts": [
        {
          "kind": "data",
          "data": {
            "auth_credentials": {
              "accessToken": "eyJhbGciOiJSUzI1NiIsInR5cCI6IkpXVCJ9..."
            }
          }
        }
      ]
    }
  }
}
```

## Challenge Response Format

If no secondary authentication token is found, the policy returns an `auth-required` A2A task
response. The response preserves the `taskId` and `contextId` from the request if originally
provided:

```json
{
  "jsonrpc": "2.0",
  "id": "req-18",
  "result": {
    "id": "task-123",
    "contextId": "ctx-456",
    "status": {
      "state": "auth-required",
      "message": {
        "kind": "message",
        "role": "agent",
        "messageId": "msg-abc123",
        "taskId": "task-123",
        "parts": [
          {
            "kind": "text",
            "text": "To continue this task, additional authorization is required."
          },
          {
            "kind": "data",
            "data": {
              "authChallenge": {
                "secondaryAuthProvider": "okta",
                "authorizationEndpoint": "https://dev-12345.okta.com/oauth2/v1/authorize",
                "tokenEndpoint": "https://dev-12345.okta.com/oauth2/v1/token",
                "scopes": ["openid", "profile", "email"],
                "audience": "https://my-agent.example.com/api",
                "redirectUri": "https://myapp.example.com/oauth/callback",
                "responseType": "code",
                "codeChallengeMethod": "S256",
                "bodyEncoding": "form"
              }
            }
          }
        ],
        "metadata": {}
      },
      "timestamp": "2025-01-01T00:00:00.000Z"
    },
    "kind": "task"
  }
}
```

## Token caching

When the request body includes a secondary access token, the policy stores it for later reuse. When
the body doesnât include a token, the policy tries the cache before returning an `auth-required`
challenge:

- The cache is keyed per user and is derived from the bearer JWT in the inbound `Authorization`
  header. The policy uses the first available of the `sub`, `email`, `azp`, or OAuth client
  identifier claims. If that JWT canât be parsed or none of these claims is present, the policy does
  not cache or reuse tokens.
- Cache entry lifetime is derived from the token timeout. When a token expires, itâs discarded from
  the cache.

- [Outbound Policies Directory](policies-outbound-directory)
- [Credential Injection OAuth 2.0](policies-outbound-oauth)
- [A2A Agent Card Policy](policies-included-a2a-agent-card)
- [A2A Schema Validation Policy](policies-included-a2a-schema-validation)

# Credential Injection API Key Policy

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><tbody><tr><td><p><strong>Policy Name</strong></p></td><td><p>Credential Injection API Key</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Summary</strong></p></td><td><p>Injects an API key or client credentials headers into outgoing requests</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Category</strong></p></td><td><p>Security</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>First Omni Gateway version available</strong></p></td><td><p>v1.10.2</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Returned Status Codes</strong></p></td><td><p>No return codes exist for this policy. Error codes are returned from the upstream service.</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

## Summary

The Credential Injection API Key policy injects authentication credentials into upstream requests to
authenticate with upstream services that require API key or client credential authentication. The
policy supports two authentication types:

- **API Key**: Injects an API key value into the `Authorization` header or a custom header.
- **Client ID and Secret**: Injects the client ID and client secret value into the `client_id` and
  `client_secret` headers or custom headers.

## Configuring Policy Parameters

### Omni Gateway Local Mode

When you apply the policy via declarative configuration files, Refer to the following policy
definition and table of parameters:

\- policyRef: name: credential-injection-api-key-flex config: authType: <string> // REQUIRED
overwrite: <boolean> // REQUIRED, default: true # For API Key authentication: apiKey: <string> //
REQUIRED when authType is "apiKey" customHeader: <string> // OPTIONAL when authType is "apiKey" #
For Client Credentials authentication: clientId: <string> // REQUIRED when authType is
"clientCredentials" clientSecret: <string> // REQUIRED when authType is "clientCredentials"
customClientIdHeader: <string> // OPTIONAL when authType is "clientCredentials"
customClientSecretHeader: <string> // OPTIONAL when authType is "clientCredentials"

<table><colgroup><col> <col> <col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Parameter</th><th>Required or Optional</th><th>Default Value</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><div><div><p><code>authType</code></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Required</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p><code>apiKey</code></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Authentication type to inject, either <code>apiKey</code> or <code>clientCredentials</code>.</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><div><div><p><code>overwrite</code></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Required</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p><code>true</code></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Overwrite authentication headers if present in the request.</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><div><div><p><code>apiKey</code></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Required when <code>authType</code> is <code>apiKey</code>.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>N/A</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>The API key value to inject.</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><div><div><p><code>customHeader</code></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Optional</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>N/A</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Use a custom header name instead of <code>Authorization</code> for API key injection.</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><div><div><p><code>clientId</code></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Required when <code>authType</code> is <code>clientCredentials</code>.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>N/A</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>The client ID to inject.</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><div><div><p><code>clientSecret</code></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Required when <code>authType</code> is <code>clientCredentials</code>.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>N/A</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>The client secret to inject.</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><div><div><p><code>customClientIdHeader</code></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Optional</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>N/A</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Use a custom header instead of <code>client_id</code> for client ID injection.</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><div><div><p><code>customClientSecretHeader</code></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Optional</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>N/A</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Use a custom header instead of <code>client_secret</code> for client secret injection.</p></div></div></td></tr></tbody></table>

#### Resource Configuration Examples

- API Key:

  \- policyRef: name: credential-injection-api-key-flex config: authType: apiKey apiKey:
  my-secret-api-key overwrite: true

- API Key with Custom Header:

  \- policyRef: name: credential-injection-api-key-flex config: authType: apiKey apiKey:
  my-secret-api-key customHeader: X-API-Key overwrite: false

- Client Credentials:

  \- policyRef: name: credential-injection-api-key-flex config: authType: clientCredentials
  clientId: my-client-id clientSecret: my-client-secret overwrite: true

- Client Credentials with Custom Headers:

  \- policyRef: name: credential-injection-api-key-flex config: authType: clientCredentials
  clientId: my-client-id clientSecret: my-client-secret customClientIdHeader: X-Client-ID
  customClientSecretHeader: X-Client-Secret overwrite: false

### Managed Omni Gateway and Omni Gateway Connected Mode

When you apply the policy from the UI, the following parameters are displayed:

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Parameter</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p><strong>Authentication Type</strong></p></td><td><p>Type of authentication header to inject: <strong>API Key</strong> or <strong>Client ID and Secret</strong>.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Overwrite headers</strong></p></td><td><p>Overwrite authentication headers if present in the request.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>API Key</strong></p></td><td><p>The API key value to inject.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Custom header</strong></p></td><td><p>Use a custom header instead of Authorization for API key injection.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Client ID</strong></p></td><td><p>The client ID to inject.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Client Secret</strong></p></td><td><p>The client secret to inject.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Custom Client ID header</strong></p></td><td><p>Use a custom header instead of <code>client_id</code> for client ID injection.</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Custom Client secret header</strong></p></td><td><p>Use a custom header instead of <code>client_secret</code> for client secret injection.</p></td></tr></tbody></table>
---

# AWS Lambda Policy

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><tbody><tr><td><p><strong>Policy name</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>AWS Lambda</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Summary</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Triggers an AWS Lambda function from a standard HTTP request</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Category</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Transformation</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>First Omni Gateway version available</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>v1.9.0</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td rowspan="4"><p><strong>Returned Status Codes</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>502 - Bad Gateway, requests are blocked due to circuit breaker activation</p></div></div></td></tr></tbody></table>

## Summary

The AWS Lambda policy triggers an AWS Lambda function from a standard HTTP request. Pass the HTTP
request payload as is or wrapped in a JSON schema.

The AWS Lambda policy is an extension of the AWS Lambda filter native to Envoy. To learn more about
the AWS Lambda filter, see
[AWS Lambda Filter](https://www.envoyproxy.io/docs/envoy/latest/configuration/http/http_filters/aws_lambda_filter).
Not all AWS Lambda filter parameters are available to the AWS Lambda policy. To find what parameters
are configurable, see [Configuring Policy Parameters](#configuring-policy-parameters).

## Configuring Policy Parameters

### Omni Gateway Local Mode

When you apply the policy via declarative configuration files, Refer to the following policy
definition and table of parameters:

```yaml
- policyRef:
    name: native-aws-lambda
  config:
    arn: <string> // REQUIRED
    payloadPassthrough: <boolean> // REQUIRED
    invocationMode: <string> // REQUIRED
    authenticationMode: <string> // REQUIRED
    credentialProfile: <string> // OPTIONAL
    credentials:
      accessKeyId: <string> // OPTIONAL
      secretAccessKey: <string> // OPTIONAL
      sessionToken: <string> // OPTIONAL
```

<table><colgroup><col> <col> <col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Parameter</th><th>Required or Optional</th><th>Default Value</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><div><div><p><code>arn</code></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Required</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>N/A</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>AWS Lambda ARN in this format: <code>arn:&lt;partition&gt;:lambda:&lt;region&gt;:&lt;account-number&gt;:function:&lt;function-name&gt;</code>.</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><div><div><p><code>payloadPassthrough</code></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Required</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>false</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>By default, Omni sends the request to the AWS Lambda function wrapped in JSON. When <code>payloadPassthrough</code> is enabled, the request is sent as is.</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><div><div><p><code>invocationMode</code></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Required</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>sync</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>The way to invoke the Lambda function, either <code>sync</code> or <code>async</code>. For <code>sync</code>, Omni Gateway awaits the AWS response. For <code>async</code>, Omni Gateway doesnât wait for the response from AWS.</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><div><div><p><code>authenticationMode</code></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Required</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>N/A</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Determines the mode of authentication. Options are: <code>default</code>, uses in order credential AWS environment variables, AWS credential file, AWS roles; <code>profile</code>, profile from AWS credential file; <code>static</code>, credentials configured in the policy.</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><div><div><p><code>credentialProfile</code></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Optional</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>N/A</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Specifies the credential <code>profile</code>. Only for <code>profile</code> authentication mode.</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><div><div><p><code>credentials</code></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Optional</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>N/A</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Configures the <code>static</code> credentials object. Only for <code>static</code> authentication mode.</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><div><div><p><code>credentials.accessKeyId</code></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Required</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>N/A</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>AWS access key ID</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><div><div><p><code>credentials.secretAccessKey</code></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Required</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>N/A</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>AWS access key</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><div><div><p><code>credentials.sessionToken</code></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Optional</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>N/A</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Session token</p></div></div></td></tr></tbody></table>

#### Resource Configuration Example

The following example defines a rate limit of three requests every six seconds:

```yaml
- policyRef:
    name: native-aws-lambda
  config:
    arn: rn:&lt;partition&gt;:lambda:&lt;region&gt;:&lt;account-number&gt;:function:&lt;function-name&gt;
    payloadPassthrough: true
    invocationMode: sync
    authenticationMode: static
    credentials:
      accessKeyId: id
      secretAccessKey: secret
      sessionToken: token
```

### Managed Omni Gateway and Omni Gateway Connected Mode

When you apply the policy from the UI, the following parameters are displayed:

<table><colgroup><col> <col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Parameter</th><th>Required or Optional</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><div><div><p>The ARN of the AWS Lambda</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Required</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>AWS Lambda ARN in this format: <code>arn:&lt;partition&gt;:lambda:&lt;region&gt;:&lt;account-number&gt;:function:&lt;function-name&gt;</code>.</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><div><div><p>Payload passthrough</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Required</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>By default, Omni sends the request to the AWS Lambda function wrapped in JSON. When enabled, the request is sent as is.</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><div><div><p>Determines the way to invoke the Lambda function</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Required</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>The way to invoke the Lambda function. For <strong>Sync</strong>, Omni Gateway awaits the AWS response. For <strong>Async</strong>, Omni Gateway doesnât wait for the response from AWS.</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><div><div><p>Determines origin of credentials</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Required</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Determines the mode of authentication. Options are: <strong>Default</strong>, uses in order credential AWS environment variables, AWS credential file, AWS roles; <strong>Profile</strong>, profile from AWS credential file; <strong>Static</strong>, credentials configured in the policy.</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><div><div><p>Credentials profile</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Optional</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Specifies the credential profile. Only for <strong>Profile</strong> authentication mode.</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><div><div><p>Credentials</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Optional</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Configures the static credentials. Only for <strong>Static</strong> authentication mode.</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><div><div><p>AccessKeyId</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Required</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>AWS access key ID</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><div><div><p>SecretAccessKey</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Required</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>AWS access key</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><div><div><p>SessionToken</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Optional</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Session token</p></div></div></td></tr></tbody></table>

# Credential Injection Basic Authentication Policy

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><tbody><tr><td><p><strong>Policy Name</strong></p></td><td><p>Credential Injection Basic Authentication</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Summary</strong></p></td><td><p>Injects a basic authorization header into outgoing requests</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Category</strong></p></td><td><p>Security</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>First Omni Gateway version available</strong></p></td><td><p>v1.9.0</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Returned Status Codes</strong></p></td><td><p>No return codes exist for this policy. Error codes are returned from the upstream service.</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

## Summary

The Credential Injection Basic Authentication policy injects an `Authorization` header containing a
username and password into outbound requests. The policy ensures that requests sent from Omni
Gateway to an upstream service include the correct authentication headers. The Authorization header
format is `Authorization: Basic <username:password>`.

The policy follows [Basic HTTP authentication standards](https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc7617).

When an error occurs, the policy returns a `WWW-Authenticate` HTTP header field.

## Configuring Policy Parameters

### Omni Gateway Local Mode

When you apply the policy via declarative configuration files, Refer to the following policy
definition and table of parameters:

\- policyRef: name: credential-injection-basic-auth-flex config: username: <string> // OPTIONAL
password: <string> // OPTIONAL overwrite: <boolean> // OPTIONAL customHeader: <string> // OPTIONAL

<table><colgroup><col> <col> <col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Parameter</th><th>Required or Optional</th><th>Default Value</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><div><div><p><code>username</code></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Optional</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>N/A</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>The username for the basic authentication credentials.</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><div><div><p><code>password</code></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Optional</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>N/A</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>The password for the basic authentication credentials.</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><div><div><p><code>overwrite</code></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Optional</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>true</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>When enabled, if the <code>Authorization</code> header is present, the policy overwrites it with the configured credentials.</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><div><div><p><code>customHeader</code></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Optional</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>N/A</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>When configured, a custom header is injected instead of the <code>Authorization</code> header. If <strong>Overwrite header</strong> is enabled, the policy overwrites the custom header if present instead of the <code>Authorization</code> header.</p></div></div></td></tr></tbody></table>

#### Resource Configuration Example

\- policyRef: name: http-basic-authentication-flex config: username: chris password: admin

### Managed Omni Gateway and Omni Gateway Connected Mode

When you apply the policy from the UI, the following parameters are displayed:

<table><colgroup><col> <col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Parameter</th><th>Description</th><th>Required?</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p>Username</p></td><td><p>The username for the basic authentication credentials.</p></td><td><p>Optional</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>Password</p></td><td><p>The password for the basic authentication credentials.</p></td><td><p>Optional</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>Overwrite header</p></td><td><p>When enabled, if the <code>Authorization</code> header is present, the policy overwrites it with the configured credentials.</p></td><td><p>Optional</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>Custom Header</p></td><td><p>When configured, a custom header is injected instead of the <code>Authorization</code> header. If <strong>Overwrite header</strong> is enabled, the policy overwrites the custom header if present instead of the <code>Authorization</code> header.</p></td><td><p>Optional</p></td></tr></tbody></table>
---

# Circuit Breaker Policy

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><tbody><tr><td><p><strong>Policy name</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Circuit Breaker</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Summary</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Prevents an upstream from receiving too many requests or connections</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Category</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>Quality of Service</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>First Omni Gateway version available</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>v1.9.0</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td rowspan="4"><p><strong>Returned Status Codes</strong></p></td><td><div><div><p>503 - Service Unavailable</p></div></div></td></tr></tbody></table>

## Summary

The Circuit Breaker policy prevents upstream services from receiving too many requests or
connections at a time. If the defined maximums are reached, Omni Gateway returns a
`503 - Service Unavailable` error.

For Managed Omni Gateways or Connected Mode, each API instance has its own independent limit when
requesting the same upstream, allowing each to use the full maximum. In Local Mode, the limits are
shared among different API instances, so they must all adhere to the same upstream maximum. For all
modes, maximums are not distributed across replicas; each Gateway can use the full maximum.

The Circuit Breaker policy is an extension of the Envoy upstream clusters circuit breaking feature.
To learn more about the Envoyâs circuit breaking, see
[Circuit Breaking](https://www.envoyproxy.io/docs/envoy/latest/intro/arch_overview/upstream/circuit_breaking.html)
in the Envoy documentation.

## Configuring Policy Parameters

### Omni Gateway Local Mode

When you apply the policy via declarative configuration files, Refer to the following policy
definition and table of parameters:

apiVersion: gateway.mulesoft.com/v1alpha1 kind: PolicyBinding metadata: name: upstream-policy spec:
targetRef: Kind: Service name: upstream policyRef: name: circuit-breaker-flex config: thresholds:
maxConnections: <number> // OPTIONAL maxPendingRequests: <number> // OPTIONAL maxRequests: <number>
// OPTIONAL maxRetries: <number> // OPTIONAL maxConnectionPools: <number> // OPTIONAL

> [!NOTE] For Local Mode, the limits are not independent, so different API instances share the
> maximums for an upstream.

<table><colgroup><col> <col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Parameter</th><th>Required or Optional</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><div><div><p><code>maximumConnections</code></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Optional</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Maximum number of connections per API to an upstream.</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><div><div><p><code>maximumPendingRequests</code></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Optional</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Maximum number of queued requests while waiting for a connection.</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><div><div><p><code>maximumRequests</code></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Optional</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Maximum number of outstanding requests to an upstream.</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><div><div><p><code>maximumRetries</code></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Optional</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Maximum number of outstanding retries.</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><div><div><p><code>maximumConnectionPools</code></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Optional</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Maximum number of connection pools concurrently initiated to an upstream.</p></div></div></td></tr></tbody></table>

#### Resource Configuration Example

This example defines a policy that supports a maximum of one request at a time:

apiVersion: gateway.mulesoft.com/v1beta1 kind: Configuration metadata: name: circuit-breaker
namespace: test-ns spec: circuitBreaker: thresholds: maxConnections: 1 maxPendingRequests: 1
maxRequests: 1 maxRetries: 1 maxConnectionPools: 1

### Managed Omni Gateway and Omni Gateway Connected Mode

When you apply the policy from the UI, the following parameters are displayed:

<table><colgroup><col> <col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Parameter</th><th>Required or Optional</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><div><div><p>Maximum connections</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Optional</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Maximum number of connections per API to an upstream.</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><div><div><p>Maximum pending requests</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Optional</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Maximum number of queued requests while waiting for a connection.</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><div><div><p>Maximum requests</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Optional</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Maximum number of outstanding requests to an upstream.</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><div><div><p>Maximum retries</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Optional</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Maximum number of outstanding retries.</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><div><div><p>Maximum connection pools</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Optional</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Maximum number of connection pools concurrently initiated to an upstream.</p></div></div></td></tr></tbody></table>

> [!NOTE] For Managed Omni Gateways or Connected Mode, each API instance has its own independent
> limit when requesting the same upstream, allowing each to use the full maximum.

# Credential Injection JWT Generation Policy

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><tbody><tr><td><p><strong>Policy Name</strong></p></td><td><p>Credential Injection JWT Generation</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Summary</strong></p></td><td><p>Generates and injects a JWT token into outgoing requests</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Category</strong></p></td><td><p>Security</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>First Omni Gateway version available</strong></p></td><td><p>v1.12.0</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Returned Status Codes</strong></p></td><td><p>No return codes exist for this policy. Error codes are returned from the upstream service.</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

## Summary

The Credential Injection JWT Generation policy generates and injects a JWT token into outgoing
requests. It signs the token with the configured key and algorithm and supports standard time-based
claims, such as `exp`, `iat`, and `nbf`, in addition to custom claims and JWT headers. Each claim
and header name and value can be a string or a DataWeave expression. Expressions can reference
request attributes or authentication data.

## Configuring Policy Parameters

### Omni Gateway Local Mode

When you apply the policy via declarative configuration files, Refer to the following policy
definition and table of parameters:

\- policyRef: name: jwt-generation-flex config: signingMethod: <string> // REQUIRED, default: RS256
privateKey: <string> // REQUIRED iat: <boolean> // REQUIRED, default: true exp: <number> //
REQUIRED, default: 0 nbf: <number> // REQUIRED, default: 0 customClaims: // REQUIRED (can be empty
array) - name: <string> value: <string> customHeaders: // REQUIRED (can be empty array) - name:
<string> value: <string>

<table><colgroup><col> <col> <col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Parameter</th><th>Required or Optional</th><th>Default Value</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><div><div><p><code>signingMethod</code></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Required</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p><code>RS256</code></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>The algorithm that signs the JWT. Supported values: <code>RS256</code>, <code>RS384</code>, <code>RS512</code>, <code>HS256</code>, <code>HS384</code>, <code>HS512</code>, <code>ES256</code>, and <code>ES384</code>.</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><div><div><p><code>privateKey</code></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Required</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>N/A</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>The key that signs the JWT. Use PEM-formatted pkcs8 for RSA and ES. Use plain text for HS.</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><div><div><p><code>iat</code></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Required</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p><code>true</code></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>A Boolean value specifying whether to include the issued-at (iat) claim in the JWT.</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><div><div><p><code>exp</code></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Required</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p><code>0</code></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>The number of seconds from the issue time until expiration. To omit the <code>exp</code> claim, enter <code>0</code>.</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><div><div><p><code>nbf</code></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Required</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p><code>0</code></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>The number of seconds from the issue time until the token becomes valid. To omit the <code>nbf</code> claim, enter <code>0</code>.</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><div><div><p><code>customClaims</code></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Required</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>N/A</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>The claims to include in the JWT. Each entry has a <code>name</code> and a <code>value</code>. Each can be a string or a DataWeave expression. The array can be empty.</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><div><div><p><code>customHeaders</code></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Required</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>N/A</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>The JWT header parameters to include. Each entry has a <code>name</code> and a <code>value</code>. Each can be a string or a DataWeave expression. The array can be empty.</p></div></div></td></tr></tbody></table>

#### Resource Configuration Examples

- HMAC-signed JWT with custom claim:

  \- policyRef: name: jwt-generation-flex config: signingMethod: HS256 privateKey: my-secret-key
  iat: true exp: 3600 nbf: 0 customClaims: - name: "sub" value:
  "#\[authentication.properties.clientId default 'anonymous'\]" customHeaders: \[\]

- RSA-signed JWT with custom claims and headers:

  \- policyRef: name: jwt-generation-flex config: signingMethod: RS256 privateKey: | -----BEGIN
  PRIVATE KEY----- <your-private-key> -----END PRIVATE KEY----- iat: true exp: 7200 nbf: 0
  customClaims: - name: "aud" value: "'https://upstream.example.com'" - name: "client_id" value:
  "#\[authentication.properties.clientId\]" customHeaders: - name: "kid" value: "'key-1'"

### Managed Omni Gateway and Omni Gateway Connected Mode

When you apply the policy from the UI, the following parameters are displayed:

<table><colgroup><col> <col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Parameter</th><th>Description</th><th>Required?</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><p>Signing Method</p></td><td><p>The algorithm that signs the JWT. Valid values are RS256, RS384, RS512, HS256, HS384, HS512, ES256, and ES384.</p></td><td><p>Required</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>Private Key</p></td><td><p>The key that signs the JWT. Use PEM with pkcs8 for RSA and ES. Use plain text for HS.</p></td><td><p>Required</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>Issued At (iat)</p></td><td><p>A Boolean value specifying whether to include the issued-at (<code>iat</code>) claim in the JWT.</p></td><td><p>Required</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>Expiration (exp)</p></td><td><p>The number of seconds from the issue time until expiration. To omit the <code>exp</code> claim, enter <code>0</code>.</p></td><td><p>Required</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>Not Before (nbf)</p></td><td><p>The number of seconds from the issue time until the token becomes valid. To omit the <code>nbf</code> claim, enter <code>0</code>.</p></td><td><p>Required</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>Custom Claims</p></td><td><p>The claims to include in the JWT. Each entry has a <code>name</code> and a <code>value</code>. Each can be a string or a DataWeave expression. The array can be empty.</p></td><td><p>Required</p></td></tr><tr><td><p>Custom Headers</p></td><td><p>The JWT header parameters to include. Each entry has a <code>name</code> and a <code>value</code>. Each can be a string or a DataWeave expression. The array can be empty.</p></td><td><p>Required</p></td></tr></tbody></table>
---

# OAuth 2.0 OBO Credential Injection Policy

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><tbody><tr><td><p><strong>Policy Name</strong></p></td><td><p>OAuth 2.0 OBO Credential Injection</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Summary</strong></p></td><td><p>Exchanges incoming bearer tokens using OAuth 2.0 Token Exchange (RFC 8693), Microsoft Entra ID On-Behalf-Of, or OAuth 2.0 Token Exchange with CIBA</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Category</strong></p></td><td><p>Security</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>First Omni Gateway version available</strong></p></td><td><p>v1.11.0</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Returned Status Codes</strong></p></td><td><p>No return codes exist for this policy. Error codes are returned from the upstream service.</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

## Summary

The OAuth 2.0 On-Behalf-Of (OBO) Credential Injection policy exchanges an incoming bearer token for
a new token to target specific upstream services. The policy supports OAuth 2.0 Token Exchange (RFC
8693), Microsoft Entra ID On-Behalf-Of protocols, and OAuth 2.0 Token Exchange with Client Initiated
Backchannel Authentication (CIBA). This policy is applied to outbound traffic (gateway to the
backend service) to automatically exchange the OAuth 2.0 token needed for backend services.

The policy extracts the Bearer token from the incoming requestâs `Authorization` header and sends a
token exchange request to the token endpoint. The policy then replaces the `Authorization` header
with the new token returned from the token exchange service and forwards the modified request to the
backend service.

To avoid triggering multiple requests for identical tokens, successful token exchanges are cached.
By default, the cache only shares tokens between requests to the same replica. Enable distributed
caching to share tokens between Omni Replicas. To learn more, see
[Token Caching](#token-caching-oauth-obo).

## Configuring Policy Parameters

### Omni Gateway Local Mode

The OAuth 2.0 OBO Credential Injection policy isnât supported in Local Mode.

### Managed Omni Gateway and Omni Gateway Connected Mode

When you apply the policy from the UI, the following parameters are displayed:

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Parameter</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><div><div><p>Token Exchange Flow</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Token exchange flow: <strong>OAuth 2.0 Token Exchange</strong> (RFC 8693) or <strong>Microsoft Entra ID On-Behalf-Of</strong>.</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><div><div><p>Client ID</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>OAuth 2.0 client ID for token exchange.</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><div><div><p>Client Secret</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>OAuth 2.0 client secret for token exchange.</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><div><div><p>Token Endpoint</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>OAuth 2.0 token endpoint URL.</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><div><div><p>Target Type</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Parameter type for specifying the target service. Required for OAuth 2.0 Token Exchange flow. Supported values:</p></div><div><ul><li><p>Audience: Logical identifier of the target service (default)</p></li><li><p>Resource: Physical URI of the target resource (RFC 8707)</p></li></ul></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><div><div><p>Target Value</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Target audience URI or resource URI for the exchanged token. Required for OAuth 2.0 Token Exchange flow.</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><div><div><p>Requested Token Type</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>The desired token type URI for the issued token. When omitted, the parameter is not sent and the authorization server selects the issued token type. OAuth 2.0 Token Exchange flow only.</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><div><div><p>Subject Token Type</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>The token type URI for the incoming subject token from the request <code>Authorization</code> header. By default <code>urn:ietf:params:oauth:token-type:access_token</code>. For JWT tokens, use <code>urn:ietf:params:oauth:token-type:jwt</code>. OAuth 2.0 Token Exchange flow only.</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><div><div><p>Scope</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>OAuth 2.0 scope to request. Required for Microsoft Entra OBO (for example, <code>api://downstream-client-id/.default</code>) and OAuth2 Token Exchange with CIBA (for example, <code>openid</code>). Optional for OAuth 2.0 Token Exchange (RFC 8693).</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><div><div><p>Timeout</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Timeout for token exchange requests in milliseconds. Default: 10000.</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><div><div><p>Enable CIBA</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Enables OAuth 2.0 Token Exchange with CIBA flow. Available only for OAuth 2.0 Token Exchange.</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><div><div><p>CIBA Backchannel Endpoint</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>The backchannel authentication endpoint (<code>bc-authorize</code>) used to initiate the CIBA flow. Required when <strong>Enable CIBA</strong> is selected.</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><div><div><p>Login Hint Claim</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>JWT claim from the incoming subject token to send as <code>login_hint</code> in the CIBA request. If no hint is specified, <code>email</code> is used.</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><div><div><p>Binding Message</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Optional human-readable message sent as <code>binding_message</code> in the CIBA request and displayed on the authentication device.</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><div><div><p>Distributed</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>When enabled, the token cache and CIBA request state are shared across Omni Gateway replicas. Enable this option when running more than one replica to avoid duplicate token exchanges or duplicate CIBA initiations.</p></div></div></td></tr></tbody></table>

## Token Caching

The policy stores each exchanged downstream access token and reuses it when later requests present
the same token. Cached entries are tied to a stable key and expire in line with the tokenâs
lifetime:

- **TTL**: Cache entry lifetime is derived from the exchanged token.
- If the policy cannot determine expiry, it skips caching for that response. If the policy cannot
  build a cache key, it performs the exchange without using the cache.
- When **Enable CIBA** is enabled, CIBA initiation and polling state, including the approved access
  token, is cached through the same mechanism.

## CIBA Behavior

When **Enable CIBA** is selected, the policy initiates a CIBA backchannel authentication request on
the first matching outbound call and returns an authorization pending response until user
authentication is approved by the authorization server. After approval, the policy injects the
exchanged access token into the `Authorization` header and forwards requests to the backend service.

While waiting for user authentication, the policy returns a `401 Unauthorized` response with the
following JSON body:

HTTP/1.1 401 Unauthorized Retry-After: 5 Content-Type: application/json

{ Â Â "error": "authorization_pending", Â Â "error_description": "CIBA authorization flow initiated.
Downstream user authorization is in progress. Retry after the indicated interval." }
---

# Credential Injection OAuth 2.0 Policy

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><tbody><tr><td><p><strong>Policy Name</strong></p></td><td><p>Credential Injection OAuth 2.0</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Summary</strong></p></td><td><p>Injects an OAuth 2.0 authorization header into outgoing requests</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Category</strong></p></td><td><p>Security</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>First Omni Gateway version available</strong></p></td><td><p>v1.9.0</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Returned Status Codes</strong></p></td><td><p>No return codes exist for this policy. Error codes are returned from the upstream service.</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

## Summary

The Credential Injection OAuth 2.0 policy injects an `Authorization` token from an OAuth 2.0 service
into a request as a header. You can use any third-party OAuth 2.0 Identify Provider (IdP), such as
Okta, AWS, Azure, or Google Cloud Platform. The policy allows for tokens based on the OAuth 2.0
Authorization Framework to be injected into requests without having to register an external IdP
server in Anypoint Platform.

## Configuring Policy Parameters

### Omni Gateway Local Mode

When you apply the policy via declarative configuration files, Refer to the following policy
definition and table of parameters:

\- policyRef: name: credential-injection-oauth2-flex config: oauthService: <string> // REQUIRED
grantType: <string> // REQUIRED, supported values: clientCredentials or password credentialLocation:
<string> // REQUIRED, supported values: body or header clientId: <string> // REQUIRED for
clientCredentials grant type clientSecret: <string> // REQUIRED for clientCredentials grant type
username: <string> // REQUIRED for password grant type password: <string> // REQUIRED for password
grant type scope: - <string> // OPTIONAL overwrite: <boolean> // REQUIRED tokenFetchTimeout:
<number> // REQUIRED allowRequestWithoutCredential: <boolean> // REQUIRED tokenRequestBodyFormat:
<string> // REQUIRED, supported values: json or urlEncoded upstreamTokenHeaderName: <string> //
OPTIONAL upstreamTokenPrefixName: <string> // OPTIONAL cachingTtl: <number> // REQUIRED
customRequestHeaders: // OPTIONAL - name: <string> value: <string>

to include in the token.</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><div><div><p><code>overwrite</code></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Required</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p><code>true</code></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>When enabled, if the policy canât retrieve the token, Omni Gateway still sends the request.</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><div><div><p><code>tokenRequestBodyFormat</code></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Required</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p><code>urlEncoded</code></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>The If 0, the policy uses the full <code>expires_in</code> value from OAuth response. If greater than 0, the policy uses the minimum of the <code>cachingTtl</code> value and <code>expires_in</code> divided by 2.</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><div><div><p><code>customRequestHeaders</code></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Optional</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>N/A</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>A list of custom headers to add to the token request. Each header must have a <code>name</code> and <code>value</code> property.</p></div></div></td></tr></tbody></table>

#### Client Credentials Configuration Example

\- policyRef: name: credential-injection-oauth2-flex config: oauthService:
<https://oauth-server/tokens> grantType: clientCredentials credentialLocation: body clientId: id
clientSecret: secret scope: - user overwrite: true tokenFetchTimeout: 20
allowRequestWithoutCredential: true tokenRequestBodyFormat: urlEncoded cachingTtl: 0

#### Password Configuration Example

\- policyRef: name: credential-injection-oauth2-flex config: oauthService:
<https://oauth-server/tokens> grantType: password credentialLocation: body clientId: id
clientSecret: secret username: myuser password: mypassword scope: - user - read overwrite: true
tokenFetchTimeout: 20 allowRequestWithoutCredential: true tokenRequestBodyFormat: urlEncoded
cachingTtl: 0

### Managed Omni Gateway and Omni Gateway Connected Mode

When you apply the policy from the UI, the following parameters are displayed:

# Upstream Idle Timeout Policy

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

<table><colgroup><col> <col></colgroup><tbody><tr><td><p><strong>Policy Name</strong></p></td><td><p>Stream Idle Timeout</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Summary</strong></p></td><td><p>Sets the maximum idle duration for a stream between the Omni Gateway and an upstream service</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Category</strong></p></td><td><p>Quality of Service</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>First Omni Gateway version available</strong></p></td><td><p>v1.9.0</p></td></tr><tr><td><p><strong>Returned Status Codes</strong></p></td><td><p>504 - Upstream request timeout</p></td></tr></tbody></table>

## Summary

The Upstream Idle Timeout policy lets you set a maximum idle duration between requests for a stream
between Omni Gateway and the upstream service. By applying the policy, you prevent idle connections
from consuming resources while simultaneously allowing persistent connections to remain open. The
timeout starts at the last request.

By default, all Omni Gateway API instances have an upstream idle timeout of 60 seconds. Apply the
policy to override the default timeout.

## Configuring Policy Parameters

### Omni Gateway Local Mode

When you apply the policy via declarative configuration files, Refer to the following policy
definition and table of parameters:

\- policyRef: name: idle-timeout-flex config: timeout: number // REQUIRED

<table><colgroup><col> <col> <col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Parameter</th><th>Required or Optional</th><th>Default Value</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><div><div><p><code>timeout</code></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Required</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>60</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Time in seconds before Omni Gateway closes the connection.</p></div></div></td></tr></tbody></table>

#### Resource Configuration Example

\- policyRef: name: idle-timeout-flex config: timeout: 3

### Managed Omni Gateway and Omni Gateway Connected Mode

When you apply the policy from the UI, the following parameters are displayed:

<table><colgroup><col> <col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Element</th><th>Description</th><th>Required</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><div><div><p>Upstream idle timeout</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Time in seconds before Omni Gateway closes the connection.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Yes</p></div></div></td></tr></tbody></table>
---

# Ordering Policies

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

To execute certain policies before others, configure the order of execution of included and custom
policies.

You can configure both included and custom policies to execute as automated policies, API-level
policies, or upstream-level policies. Automated policies are policies that are applied to all API
instances deployed on the Omni Replica and execute before API-level policies. API-level policies are
inbound policies that are only applied to a specific API instance. Upstream level policies are
outbound policies applied to specific upstreams.

For Omni Gateway running in Connected Mode, use API Manager to order policies. For Omni Gateway
running in Local Mode, use a `PolicyBinding` YAML resource to order policies.

> [!IMPORTANT] You cannot configure the execution order of the
> [Cross-Origin Resource Sharing (CORS) policy](policies-included-cors). The CORS policy executes
> before automated and API-level policies.

## Policy Execution Order

Policies can either execute on the API request, the API response, or both. Policies execute in order
during the API request and in inverse order during the API response. If a policy does not execute in
a certain direction, Omni Gateway skips the policy in that direction. For API requests, policies
execute in the order of automated policies, API-level policies, and outbound policies. The order is
inverted for the response.

For example, if you have policies ordered:

1.  Automated Policy 1
2.  Automated Policy 2
3.  API-Level Policy 1
4.  API-Level Policy 2
5.  Upstream-Level Policy 1
6.  Upstream-Level Policy 2

The policies execute in the following order on the response:

1.  Upstream-Level Policy 2
2.  Upstream-Level Policy 1
3.  API-Level Policy 2
4.  API-Level Policy 1
5.  Automated Policy 2
6.  Automated Policy 1

## Order Automated Policies in Connected Mode

You cannot configure the order of automated policies in API Manager. Automated policies execute in
the order they are applied to the API instance. Apply automated policies in the order you want them
to execute.

## Order Automated Policies in Local Mode

To apply a automated policy to Omni Gateway running in Local Mode, you must apply a resource-level
policy. By doing so, you can apply the same policy to all API instances running on the Omni Replica.

To apply a resource-level policy to all API instances, refer to
[Secure an API with an Automated Resource-Level Policy](flex-local-secure-api-with-auto-policy).

To order automated resource-level policies, edit the `order` parameter to match when you want the
policy to execute. Ensure that this order does not conflict with the order of any other policies.
For example, if you apply two automated resource-level policies with `order: 1` and `order: 2`, any
API-level policy must start at `order: 3`.

## Order Inbound API-Level Policies for Managed Omni Gateway and Connected Mode

To reorder API-level policies:

1.  Go to **Anypoint Platform > API Manager**.
2.  In **API Instances** or **Agent and Tool Instances**, click the name of the API instance whose
    policies you want to reorder.
3.  From the left navigation menu, click **Policies**.
4.  From the top of the list of the applied policies, click **Reorder policies**.
5.  From the **Reorder Applied Policies** section, use the up and down arrows to rearrange the order
    of policies.
6.  Click **Apply order**.

## Order Outbound Policies for Managed Omni Gateway and Connected Mode

To reorder outbound policies:

1.  Go to **Anypoint Platform > API Manager**.
2.  In **API Instances** or **Agent and Tool Instances**, click the name of the API instance whose
    policies you want to reorder.
3.  From the side navigation panel, click **Policies**.
4.  Click the **Outbound policies** tab.
5.  Click the more options button (![1%](_images/more-options-menu.png)) of the upstream service
    whose policies you want to reorder.
6.  Use the up and down arrows to rearrange the order of policies.
7.  Click **Apply order**.

## Order Policies in Local Mode

In Omni Gateway running in Local Mode, configure policy ordering via a `PolicyBinding` YAML
resource.

When defining a policy using a `PolicyBinding` YAML resource, edit the `order` parameter to match
when you want the policy to execute. Ensure that you do not assign the same order number to
different policies.

For information about Local Mode policy order, see the
[Declarative Configuration Reference Guide](flex-local-configuration-reference-guide#policy-binding).

## Apply Duplicate Policies

Omni Gateway supports applying duplicate policies for all deployment types. Automated, API-level,
Outbound, and resource-level policies all support duplicate policy application.

When applying duplicate policies:

- Add a label to the duplicated policies to differentiate them.
- Consider if it makes sense to apply duplicated policies.

  For example, the duplicated IP allowlist policies allow only IPs that are allowed by both
  policies. Rather than applying duplicated policies, configure the IPs in one policy.

## See Also

- [Included Policies Directory](policies-included-directory)
---

# Resource-Level Policies

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

Unless otherwise configured, policies are by default applied to the entire API. However, you can
implement an additional level of policy granularity, one in which access is controlled based on a
criteria. Policies with this granularity are called _resource-level policies_.

At the resource level of granularity, policies are applied to only those requests that match the
criteria. All policies, except the Cross-Origin Resource Sharing (CORS) policy, are resource-level.

For Omni Gateway running in Connected Mode, you configure resource-level policies in API Manager.

For information about configuring resource-level policies for Omni Gateway running in Local Mode,
refer to
[Secure an API with an Automated Resource-Level Policy](flex-local-secure-api-with-auto-policy).
---

# Configuring TLS for Outgoing Policy Requests

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

Some Omni Gateway policies make outgoing requests to HTTP and gRPC upstream services. For example,
the [OAuth 2.0 Token Introspection Policy](policies-included-oauth-token-introspection) sends token
validation requests to an OAuth 2.0 service, and the
[External Authorization Policy](policies-included-external-authorization) forwards incoming client
requests to an authorization service.

By default, Managed and Self-Managed Omni Gateways protect these connections with a default TLS
Context. The default TLS context is not mTLS and uses the default ciphers listed in
[TLS Cipher Support on Omni Gateway](#local-support).

To override the default TLS context, configure a custom default TLS context for all outgoing
requests from policies. You canât configure a custom default TLS context for Managed Omni Gateway.

## Configure a TLS Context for Self-Managed Gateways

For both local and connected mode, use a YAML configuration file to configure TLS between a policy
and an upstream service:

apiVersion: gateway.mulesoft.com/v1alpha1 kind: Configuration metadata: name: default-tls spec:
defaultTLS: outboundPolicyCalls: skipValidation: <boolean> // OPTIONAL trustedCA: <string> #
OPTIONAL, defaults to the CA of operating systems certificate: // OPTIONAL key: <string> // OPTIONAL
crt: <string> // OPTIONAL alpn: <array> // OPTIONAL minversion: <string> // OPTIONAL maxversion:
<string> // OPTIONAL ciphers: <array> // OPTIONAL

Not including optional parameters in your configuration file applies the parameters default values
to your TLS context.

When configuring the `ciphers` parameter, ensure that your API upstream supports the listed ciphers.
For the supported ciphers, see [TLS Cipher Support on Omni Gateway](#local-support).

<table><colgroup><col> <col> <col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Parameter</th><th>Required or Optional</th><th>Default Value</th><th>Description</th></tr></thead><tbody><tr><td><div><div><p><code>skipValidation</code></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Optional</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>false</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>If true, the upstream certificate is not validated.</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><div><div><p><code>trustedCA</code></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Optional</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>N/A</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>The CA used to validate the upstream server certificate. If no trusted CA is provided, the policy uses the default OS CA.</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><div><div><p><code>certificate</code></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Optional</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>N/A</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>The client certificate to present for mTLS. To comply with security standards, all certificates must be 2048 bits or longer.</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><div><div><p><code>certificate.key</code></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Optional</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>N/A</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>The private key part of the certificate.</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><div><div><p><code>certificate.crt</code></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Optional</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>N/A</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>The public key part of the certificate.</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><div><div><p><code>alpn</code></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Optional</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p><code>h2</code> and <code>http/1.1</code></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>A prioritized list of supported application level protocols; for example, h2, http/1.1, and so forth.</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><div><div><p><code>minversion</code></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Optional</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p><code>1.2</code></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>The minimum TLS version allowed.</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><div><div><p><code>maxversion</code></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Optional</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p><code>1.3</code></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>The maximum TLS version allowed.</p></div></div></td></tr><tr><td><div><div><p><code>ciphers</code></p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>Optional</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>For the default and other supported ciphers, see <a href="#local-support">TLS Cipher Support on Omni Gateway</a>.</p></div></div></td><td><div><div><p>A list of supported TLS ciphers (IANA format).</p></div></div></td></tr></tbody></table>

## TLS Cipher Support on Omni Gateway

Omni can support a range of TLS Versions from TLS 1.2 to TLS 1.3, and you can also customize some of
the ciphers to support.

You cannot customize the list of TLS 1.3 Ciphers. If you support TLS 1.3, the TLS 1.3 default
ciphers are all supported regardless of the ciphers listed in the configuration file. If you donât
support TLS 1.3, these ciphers are not included.

For TLS 1.2, listing any ciphers overides the TLS 1.2 default ciphers. If you want to list ciphers
besides the default TLS 1.2 Ciphers, you must list every cipher to support including the default
ciphers you want to support. Excluding default ciphers from your list of supported ciphers means you
do not support those ciphers. If you wish to only support the default ciphers, you can leave the
cipher list blank. Listing ciphers does not affect the default TLS 1.3 cipher.

> [!IMPORTANT] For outbound TLS Context, ensure that your upstream service supports the selected
> ciphers and versions.

### Omni Gateway Supported Ciphers

Omni Gateway supports the following TLS Ciphers in Connected Mode and Local Mode:

<table><colgroup><col> <col> <col> <col></colgroup><thead><tr><th>Cipher</th><th>TLS
